revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}} {{Multiple issues| {{copyedit|date=February 2023}} {{More citations needed|date=February 2023}} {{Expand Japanese|大王 (ヤマト王権)|date=November 2023}} }} '''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, ''Ōkimi'', ''Daiō'') or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō''') was the title of the head of the [[Yamato Kingship]] or the [[monarch]] title of [[Wakoku]] (Old Japan) from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Asuka period]] in [[History of Japan|ancient Japan]]. <!-- {{Nihongo|Okimi|大王}} or {{Nihongo|Ame no shita shiroshimesu ōkimi|治天下大王}} is the title of [[Lord mayor]] of the [[Yamato Kingship]] from the [[Kofun period]] to the [[Asuka period]], or the title of [[Monarch]] of Japan. -->
== Etymology == The title "{{lang|ja|大王}}" ('''Ōkimi''' or '''Daiō'''), which is an [[Honorific|honorific title]] for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s, when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix "ō/oho," which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title "kimi" (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that "Ōkimi" is only an honorific form of "kimi," a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of [[Sino-Japanese vocabulary|kango]] (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）項目「大王」</ref> There are many instances of the use of "{{lang|ja|大王}}", which is understood as a courtesy title of Emperor or royal family. <!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Order]] began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. -->
== Title of ''King of Japan'' in Chinese documents == === ''King'' as a title in ancient China === The [[kanji]] title "{{lang|ja|王}}" (''Ō, Wang'') originally designated a Master of ''Chūgen'' ({{lang|ja|中原}}, [[Zhongyuan]]) in [[China proper|Inner China]]. In the [[Zhou dynasty]] period, {{lang|zh|王}} (''Wang'') was the title of the sole [[Son of Heaven]] who rules the [[Tianxia]]. However, some great powers in the region of the [[Yangtze civilization]] did not want to stand in subordinate positions of the nations of the [[Yellow River civilization]] in [[North China]], such as [[Chu (state)|Chu]], [[Wu (state)|Wu]] and [[Yue (state)|Yue]]. Some of their monarchs titled themselves ''Wang''.
When China entered into the [[Warring States period]], the monarchs of the great nations among the [[Ancient Chinese states|states of North China]] who were originally subjects of the Zhou King, but achieved territorial statehood, called themselves sole ''Wang'' of the Tianxia in place of the Zhou King. Thus, there were numerous claims to the throne in mainland China. Thereafter, Ying Zheng ({{lang|zh|嬴政}}), the King of [[Qin (state)|Qin]] (Emperor [[Qin Shi Huang|Shi Huang]]), who unified China for the first time in 221 BC, adopted the title "Emperor" ({{lang|zh|皇帝}}, ''Huángdì'') instead of title "King", which had been degraded. The King title ''Wang'' became the title granted to subjects of the Emperor, or that assigned to heads of neighboring states who recognized the authority of the Qin Emperor as Master of Tianxia, with a connotation of a subordinate rank. The latter usage was established thereafter. The monarch of [[Xiongnu]] stood on even ground with the Emperor of Qin, therefore his title was [[Chanyu]], not ''Wang''.
=== Early title of ''King of Japan'' === {{unsourced|section|date=November 2023}}
The first appearance of King title related to old Japan is {{lang|ja|漢委奴国王}} (''Kan no Wi no Na no kokuō'', King of Na in Wi of [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Han]]) engraved on the [[King of Na gold seal|gold seal]] which was bestowed on the king of [[Nakoku]] by the emperor [[Emperor Guangwu of Han|Guangwu]] of Han in 57 AD.
The word ''Wakoku ō'' (King of Wa) appears in the article dated to the first year of ''Eisho'' (107 AD), in the record of [[Emperor An of Han|Emperor An]] in the ''[[Book of the Later Han]]''. The full title, as written in the record of emperor An, is: "[[Suishō]], the king of Wa, and other". If "Wakoku ō" refers to the King of [[Wakoku]] as a head of the states union, other than a head of small regional state, this description shows the establishment of the [[Wakoku]].
In addition, [[Himiko]] (c. 180 AD–c. 247 AD) was authorised as the unified Queen of Wakoku (whose capital was [[Yamatai|Yamatai koku]]) by the [[Cao Wei|Wei]] dynasty. There is a theory that the government that existed during Himiko's rule was an early form of the [[Yamato Kingship]], though this has been disputed. <!--
== Japanese king titles found in Chinese literature == Originally, the term "king" referred to the "Lord of [[Zhongyuan|Chuanyuan]]" in [[China proper|mainland China]]. The [[Zhou dynasty]] had the title of king as the only [[Son of Heaven]] to rule [[Tianxia]], but the [[Yellow River civilization]] of [[North China]] However, some of the great powers of the Yangtze River civilization, which did not take kindly to standing in the wind of the [[Yellow River civilization]] countries of [[North China]], had their monarchs take the title of king, such as [[Chu (state)|Chuxia]], [[Wu (state)|Wu]], and [[Yue (state)|Yue]]. In the [[Warring States period]], the monarchs of the great states that achieved territorial statehood among the [[Ancient Chinese states|Lords]], the vassals of King Zhou, began to call themselves the only "kings" under heaven, replacing King Zhou. Later, when [[Qin Shi Huang|Qin King Qin Ying Zheng]] (the First Emperor) unified the Chinese world for the first time (221 BC), he used "emperor" instead of "king" which had lost its value. Later, when [[Qin (state)|Qin]] was established ([[221 BC]]), "king" became a title given to the emperor's vassals (princes and kings) or to the heads of neighboring states who recognized the emperor's authority under heaven and showed their subservience to him (thus, for example, [[Xiongnu]], the equal of Han). Xiongnu]], the sovereign of the [[Xiongnu]], which was the counterpart of the Han Dynasty, was [[Chanyu|Single Yu]] and not a king).
The first appearance of a king title related to Japan is "[[King of Na gold seal|King of Han]]" engraved on the gold seal that [[Emperor Guangwu of Han|Guangwu Emperor]] gave to the king of [[Nakoku]] in [[AD 57|57]].
Next, the word "King of Japan" can be seen for the first time in the article of the first year of the reign of Emperor An in the [[Book of the Later Han]] ([[AD 107|107]]). As the An Emperor's Chronicle states, "King of Japan [[Suishō]], etc.," this indicates the establishment of the nation of Japan, considering that the King of Japan was not a small regional state, but the "King of Japan" as the head of a regional federation of states.
Then, in a slightly lower period, [[Himiko]] (died 240 - 249) was also recognized by [[Cao Wei|Wei]] as the united queen of Japan (the capital was [[Yamatai|Jamataikoku]]). There are theories (some disagree) that this regime of Himiko was the first [[Yamato Kingship]].
-->
== Establishment of the ''Daiō (Great King)'' expression == It has been theorized{{by whom|date=November 2023}} that [[Emperor Ingyō]], who is identified with Sai/Sei ({{lang|ja|濟}}), one of the [[Five kings of Wa]], is supposed to be the {{lang|ja|王}} (King) which is appeared in the inscription {{lang|ja|王賜□□□敬□}} on the {{ill|Ōshimei iron sword|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} unearthed from the {{ill|Inaridai 1st Kofun|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} in [[Chiba prefecture]]. If this is the case, then Ingyō may not be {{lang|ja|大王}} (great king), but king.{{original research inline|date=November 2023}} As for King Wakatakeru ([[Emperor Yūryaku]]), who is identified{{by whom|date=November 2023}} with King Bu ({{lang|ja|武}}), there is the inscription {{lang|ja|獲加多支鹵大王}} on an iron sword unearthed in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] in [[Saitama prefecture]], as well as the inscription {{lang|ja|治天下獲□□□鹵大王}} on an iron sword unearthed in the [[Eta Funayama Kofun]] in [[Kumamoto prefecture]]. From these inscriptions, it is theorized{{by whom|date=November 2023}} that Yūryaku might have used the title "Chi Tenka Daiō" ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}) inside Japan. It also suggests the title "Chi Tenka Daiō" was first used in the late 5th century.
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the [[Nara period]], there are the scripts "{{lang|ja|大王、風姿…}}" (Daiō, fūshi...) in the first Enthronement record of Ohosazaki no Sumeramikoto ([[Emperor Nintoku]])<!--大鷦鷯天皇（おほさざきのすめらみこと）-->, but it is not certain that this ''Daiō'' had been used from the days of Emperor Nintoku, who ruled some 200 years before the compilation of the ''Nihon Shoki''. However, the expression of ''Daiō'' appears in the [[Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] record, and after that, it appears in the [[Emperor Ingyō|Ingyō]] record, [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] record, [[Emperor Kenzō|Kenzō]] record and [[Emperor Keitai|Keitai]] record, among others.
There is the inscription {{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 男弟王 在意紫沙加宮時 斯麻 念長寿 遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱 作此鏡」}} (according to Toshio Fukuyama) <ref>{{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 …… 作此鏡」}} can be read roughly: "''In the year of {{lang|ja|癸未}} (Kibi), August, Day 10, Great King (Daiō) year .... this Mirror is made ({{lang|ja|作此鏡}})''". </ref> on the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] (Portrait of person Mirror), owned by [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], [[Wakayama prefecture]], in which the words {{lang|ja|大王}} and {{lang|ja|男弟王}} appear. From this inscription, in the year ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' ({{lang|ja|癸未}}, ''Kibi'') <ref>See "[[Sexagenary cycle]]". ''Kibi (Mizunoto Hitsuji)'' is the name of year (year of "Water Goat"). There are 60 names of year according to Sexagenary cycle (''Kanshi''). Named year exists in each 60 years. See also [[:ja:%E7%99%B8%E6%9C%AA|ja:Mizunoto Hitsuji]] or [[:fr:Ch%C3%A8vre_d%27eau|French article]].</ref> when the mirror was made, the title ''Daiō'' is supposed to have been used. But there are various interpretations of what year this ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' year was. In several theories, it is: 383, 443, 503, or 623 AD. Among these years, 443 ([[Emperor Ingyō]]) or 503 ([[Emperor Buretsu]]) are considered most likely. If it is 443, the expression Daiō was used in around the age of Ingyō, in the middle 5th century. However, the characters in the inscription are difficult to interpret. In addition, the content of the inscription itself has various interpretations. The exact years when the expression ''Daiō'' started to be used are unclear.
<!-- skipped part exists HERE
--> In the [[Man'yōshū]], the expression {{lang|ja|大王}} is the most common, and 57 examples appear. Other expressions {{lang|ja|王}}, {{lang|ja|皇}}, {{lang|ja|大王}}, and {{lang|ja|大皇}} also appear. However, all these words are read "Ōkimi" or "Ohokimi", and there are no examples that the single character {{lang|ja|王}} is read "Kimi". The word {{lang|ja|大君}} is rarely used. There are only a few examples, such as the line {{lang|ja|やすみしし我が大君…}} (Yasumishi shi waga ōkimi...) by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]].<!-- omitted -- 万葉集には「大王」の表記が最も多く57例が見られ、他に「王」「皇」「大王」「大皇」が見られる。ただしいずれも“オオキミ”“オホキミ”と訓ませており、「王」を単一で“キミ”と訓む例はない。“キミ”に権威を背景とする君主を意味する「君」の字を当てた「大君」は万葉集には柿本人麻呂が詠んだ「やすみしし我が大君…」など数例が見えるのみである。橋本達雄は、記紀歌謡・万葉歌双方に見られるの表現を考察して、枕詞の「やすみしし」は全世界を統治する意で、天皇の称号と同様に道教の教理に基づいて案出されたものと説いている。--> <!--
There is a theory that [[Emperor Ingyō]], who is identified with {{lang|und|Sai or Sei|italics=no}} ({{lang|lzh|濟}}) of the ancient Japanese [[Five kings of Wa]], is the ''king'' in the inscription of the [[Inaridai Sword]], whose gift the sword is, according to its inscription: {{lang|lzh|王賜□□}}, ('[the] king bestows ..'). There is a theory that it was not the "Great King".{{explain|date=February 2022}}
In the case of King Wakatake ([[Emperor Yūryaku]], or Emperor Yusei), who is compared to [[Takeshi]], the inscription on [[Inariyama Sword]], an iron burial weapon excavated from the [[Inariyama Kofun]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]], reads, {{lang|und|Kari-Katashike Daio}}.{{meaning|date=February 2022}} The inscription on an iron sword excavated in the [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], from the [[Eta Funayama Kofun]] reads, {{lang|und|Osamu Tenka Kari}}{{lang|lzh|□□□□鹵大王}}. This suggests that the title {{lang|und|Ji Tenka Daioh}} was born around this time, the late 5th century.
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the Nara period (710-794), the title ''Daiō, fūshiki...'' appears in the period before the accession of Emperor Dōgi (''Emperor Nintoku''), but it is not clear whether the title was used from the time of Emperor Nintoku, long before its compilation. The word "daiō" first appears in the Ojinki, and then in the Yunkyōki, Yūryūki, Kensōki, and Jūtaiki.
The inscription on a "Personality Mirror", the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] in the collection of Sumida Hachiman Shrine in [[Wakayama Prefecture]] reads, "On the tenth day of the eighth month of the eighth year of the year of the King, when the King's younger brother was at the palace of Zishaga, he sent the two men of the Kaisaka Faijai to take the white upper and the same 200 droughts and made this mirror." (by Toshio Fukuyama). This mirror was made in the same way as the other mirrors. From this, it can be inferred that the title "Great King" was used in the year of the 60th year of the reign when the mirror was made, but there are several theories on the interpretation of the year of the 60th year of the reign, including AD 383, 443, 503, and 623. However, the exact date of use of the "Daewoong" inscription is not known. However, the exact date of use of the "Daewoong" inscription is not clear, as there are many characters that are not readily interpreted, including variants of the chronological The "Daewoong" inscription is not clear, as there are many characters that are not readily interpreted, including variants of the chronological
temporarily skipped
In the Sui Dynasty (581-618), the first mission to Sui China in the 20th year of the reign of Emperor Kai (600), described in the Sui Dynasty Book of Records as "{{Lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}". The name of the king of China, Tarishibekko, is thought to have been "Ahakemi," which means "great god". In the second [[Japanese missions to Sui China]] of the 3rd year of the reign of Emperor Daigyo (607), the title "Son of Heaven" was used in the national epistle, "{{Lang|zh| 日出處天子致書日沒處天子無恙云}}". However, it is thought that the "Ookimi" (Great King) and the "Amae-no-shitashiro-shime-su Ookimi" (Great King) were used in Japan.
In the inscription on the halo of the statue of Yakushi Nyorai in the Golden Hall of [[Hōryū-ji]], built by [[Prince Shōtoku]] in the early [[7th century]], the names "Emperor Yōmei" (Great King), [[Emperor Suiko]], and the "Aeme-no-shitashiro-shime-su Ookimi" (Great King) were used in Japan, suggesting that the title "Ame-no-shita-shironoshime-no-oh" was used (Suiko dynasty theory). However, the inscription itself uses words such as "Emperor" and "Holy King of the Eastern Palace" as well as Japanese expressions such as {{lang|ja|Dai Goshimono Teshigaki}}, suggesting that it is too early to be from the Suiko period, and the style of the inscription has an early [[Tang dynasty]] feel to it. In addition to the theory of the Suiko dynasty, there is another theory that the statue was made in the Suiko period. In addition to the Suiko dynasty theory, the Tenchi dynasty theory and the Tembu and Jito dynasties theory are also known.
skipped
In the [[Man'yōshū]], "daiō" is the most common spelling, with 57 examples, and other spellings such as "o", "ō", "daiō", and "daiō" are also found. However, in all of these cases, the word is pronounced as "oh-kimi" or "oh-kimi," and there is no single example of the word "king" being pronounced as "kimi". There are only a few examples in the Man'yōshū of "Ookimi," in which the character for "kimi" is applied to the character for "kun," meaning a sovereign with a background of authority, such as in the poem "''Good night, my great sovereign...''" by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] (Kakinomoto Maro). Hashimoto Tatsuo, examining the expressions found in both Kiki and Manyo poems, argues that the pillow word {{lang|und|yasumi-shi}} means 'to rule the whole world', and was conceived based on Taoist doctrine as well as the emperor's title.
--> <!-- errors so many hidden / corrections are needed
== Formation of "Emperor" == It is widely known that the notation '''[[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]'''' originates from the ''Emperor of Japan'', who is regarded as the ''supreme god of the universe'' in [[Taoism]] brought from the continent.
In the [[Emperor of Japan]], it is said to have been first published in the [[Nihon Shoki]] in September of the 16th year of the [[Empress Suiko|Suiko]] reign, "The Emperor of the East respectfully addresses the Emperor of the West. In addition, there is an article in the [[Tenjukoku Shūchō Mandala]]<ref>{{Cite journal|last=千田稔|year=2007|title=「天皇」号成立推古朝説の系譜 : もう一つの邪馬台国論争的状況|url=https://doi.org/10.15055/00000573|journal=日本研究|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|volume=35|pages=405–419|doi=10.15055/00000573|issn=0915-0900|access-date=2021-09-28}}</ref>、And in the preface to [[Kaifūsō]], the emperor notation is used only for the period after [[Empress Jitō|Emperor Jitō]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=西野誠一|year=2005|title=天皇号の成立年代|url=https://hdl.handle.net/2297/1439|journal=金沢大学文学部日本史学研究室紀要|publisher=金沢大学文学部史学科日本史学研究室|issue=第1号|pages=21–32|issn=1880-2079|access-date=2021-09-28}}</ref> In recent years, the latter is the most likely explanation. In recent years, the latter theory is considered more likely.
The adoption of "emperor" as an official description of a sovereign was most likely in the [[Tenmu dynasty]]. It is speculated that [[Emperor Tenmu|Tenmu]] may have followed the example of [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong]] of the [[Tang dynasty]], who changed "emperor" to "emperor" in [[674]]. The name "Emperor" is the name of the supreme god of the universe in ancient China, [[Shangdi|tian emperor]], and is closely related to [[Taoism]].
Shavings of [[Mokkan]], in which characters such as "Otsu-ko", "Tsu-ko", and "Prince" can be seen, have been excavated from the Asuka-kyo site. These have been interpreted to refer to [[Prince Ōtsu|Prince Otsu]], the son of Tenmu, and are thought to date from the 10th year of Tenmu ([[681]]) based on other wooden strips excavated at the same time.The fact that the prince notation was used in the 10th year of the Tenmu Era is thought to be proof that the emperor notation was used before that.
In addition, "Sumeramikoto" and "Sumeroki" are used for the word "emperor. There was a theory that "Sumeru" was a corruption of "to rule", but this theory was rejected by [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]], and it is still unclear today.{{Efn|Another theory is that it is a corruption of the word "clear," which evokes sanctity from cleanliness, and that it is a corruption of the word "emperor," which expresses a shining and sparkling appearance.}}<ref name="kokugakuin2">{{Cite web |date=2014-04-29 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68627 |access-date=2022-04-01 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140429194813/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68627 |archive-date=29 April 2014 |url-status=dead}}</ref>。In the Manyoshu, there are twelve known instances of the word "emperor," of which seven are "Okimi" and five are "Sumeroki. From a comparison of the meanings of the two words, it is clear that "Okimi" is used for the emperor of the day, while "Sumeroki" is used for past [[List of emperors of Japan|the emperors of the past]] and the god of the emperors. It is known that "Okimi" is used for the current emperor and "Sumeroki" is used for past [[List of emperors of Japan]]<ref name="kokugakuin">{{Cite web |date=2016-03-05 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68630 |access-date=2022-04-01 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305231304/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68630 |archive-date=5 March 2016 |url-status=dead}}</ref>。 -->

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}} {{Multiple issues| {{copyedit|date=February 2023}} {{More citations needed|date=February 2023}} {{Expand Japanese|大王 (ヤマト王権)|date=November 2023}} }} '''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, ''Ōkimi'', ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''), was the title of the head of the [[Yamato Kingship]], or the [[monarch]] title of [[Wakoku]] (Old Japan). This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Asuka period]] in [[History of Japan|ancient Japan]]. <!-- {{Nihongo|Okimi|大王}} or {{Nihongo|Ame no shita shiroshimesu ōkimi|治天下大王}} is the title of [[Lord mayor]] of the [[Yamato Kingship]] from the [[Kofun period]] to the [[Asuka period]], or the title of [[Monarch]] of Japan. -->
== Etymology == The title "{{lang|ja|大王}}" ('''Ōkimi''' or '''Daiō'''), which is an [[Honorific|honorific title]] for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix "ō/oho," which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title "kimi" (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that "Ōkimi" is only an honorific form of "kimi," a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of [[Sino-Japanese vocabulary|kango]] (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）項目「大王」</ref> There are many instances of the use of "{{lang|ja|大王}}", which is understood as a courtesy title of Emperor or royal family. <!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Order]] began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. -->
== Title of ''King of Japan'' in Chinese documents == === ''King'' as a title in ancient China === The [[kanji]] title "{{lang|ja|王}}" (''Ō, Wang'') originally designated a Master of ''Chūgen'' ({{lang|ja|中原}}, [[Zhongyuan]]) in [[China proper|Inner China]]. In the [[Zhou dynasty]] period, {{lang|zh|王}} (''Wang'') was the title of the sole [[Son of Heaven]] who rules the [[Tianxia]]. However, some great powers in the region of the [[Yangtze civilization]] did not want to stand in subordinate positions of the nations of the [[Yellow River civilization]] in [[North China]], such as [[Chu (state)|Chu]], [[Wu (state)|Wu]] and [[Yue (state)|Yue]]. Some of their monarchs titled themselves ''Wang''.
When China entered into the [[Warring States period]], the monarchs of the great nations among the [[Ancient Chinese states|states of North China]] who were originally subjects of the Zhou King, but achieved territorial statehood, called themselves sole ''Wang'' of the Tianxia in place of the Zhou King. Thus, there were numerous claims to the throne in mainland China. Thereafter, Ying Zheng ({{lang|zh|嬴政}}), the King of [[Qin (state)|Qin]] (Emperor [[Qin Shi Huang|Shi Huang]]), who unified China for the first time in 221 BC, adopted the title "Emperor" ({{lang|zh|皇帝}}, ''Huángdì'') instead of title "King", which had been degraded. The King title ''Wang'' became the title granted to subjects of the Emperor, or that assigned to heads of neighboring states who recognized the authority of the Qin Emperor as Master of Tianxia, with a connotation of a subordinate rank. The latter usage was established thereafter. The monarch of [[Xiongnu]] stood on even ground with the Emperor of Qin, therefore his title was [[Chanyu]], not ''Wang''.
=== Early title of ''King of Japan'' === {{unsourced|section|date=November 2023}}
The first appearance of King title related to old Japan is {{lang|ja|漢委奴国王}} (''Kan no Wi no Na no kokuō'', King of Na in Wi of [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Han]]) engraved on the [[King of Na gold seal|gold seal]] which was bestowed on the king of [[Nakoku]] by the emperor [[Emperor Guangwu of Han|Guangwu]] of Han in 57 AD.
The word ''Wakoku ō'' (King of Wa) appears in the article dated to the first year of ''Eisho'' (107 AD), in the record of [[Emperor An of Han|Emperor An]] in the ''[[Book of the Later Han]]''. The full title, as written in the record of emperor An, is: "[[Suishō]], the king of Wa, and other". If "Wakoku ō" refers to the King of [[Wakoku]] as a head of the states union, other than a head of small regional state, this description shows the establishment of the [[Wakoku]].
In addition, [[Himiko]] (c. 180 AD–c. 247 AD) was authorised as the unified Queen of Wakoku (whose capital was [[Yamatai|Yamatai koku]]) by the [[Cao Wei|Wei]] dynasty. There is a theory that the government that existed during Himiko's rule was an early form of the [[Yamato Kingship]], though this has been disputed. <!--
== Japanese king titles found in Chinese literature == Originally, the term "king" referred to the "Lord of [[Zhongyuan|Chuanyuan]]" in [[China proper|mainland China]]. The [[Zhou dynasty]] had the title of king as the only [[Son of Heaven]] to rule [[Tianxia]], but the [[Yellow River civilization]] of [[North China]] However, some of the great powers of the Yangtze River civilization, which did not take kindly to standing in the wind of the [[Yellow River civilization]] countries of [[North China]], had their monarchs take the title of king, such as [[Chu (state)|Chuxia]], [[Wu (state)|Wu]], and [[Yue (state)|Yue]]. In the [[Warring States period]], the monarchs of the great states that achieved territorial statehood among the [[Ancient Chinese states|Lords]], the vassals of King Zhou, began to call themselves the only "kings" under heaven, replacing King Zhou. Later, when [[Qin Shi Huang|Qin King Qin Ying Zheng]] (the First Emperor) unified the Chinese world for the first time (221 BC), he used "emperor" instead of "king" which had lost its value. Later, when [[Qin (state)|Qin]] was established ([[221 BC]]), "king" became a title given to the emperor's vassals (princes and kings) or to the heads of neighboring states who recognized the emperor's authority under heaven and showed their subservience to him (thus, for example, [[Xiongnu]], the equal of Han). Xiongnu]], the sovereign of the [[Xiongnu]], which was the counterpart of the Han Dynasty, was [[Chanyu|Single Yu]] and not a king).
The first appearance of a king title related to Japan is "[[King of Na gold seal|King of Han]]" engraved on the gold seal that [[Emperor Guangwu of Han|Guangwu Emperor]] gave to the king of [[Nakoku]] in [[AD 57|57]].
Next, the word "King of Japan" can be seen for the first time in the article of the first year of the reign of Emperor An in the [[Book of the Later Han]] ([[AD 107|107]]). As the An Emperor's Chronicle states, "King of Japan [[Suishō]], etc.," this indicates the establishment of the nation of Japan, considering that the King of Japan was not a small regional state, but the "King of Japan" as the head of a regional federation of states.
Then, in a slightly lower period, [[Himiko]] (died 240 - 249) was also recognized by [[Cao Wei|Wei]] as the united queen of Japan (the capital was [[Yamatai|Jamataikoku]]). There are theories (some disagree) that this regime of Himiko was the first [[Yamato Kingship]].
-->
== Establishment of the ''Daiō (Great King)'' expression == It has been theorized{{by whom|date=November 2023}} that [[Emperor Ingyō]], who is identified with Sai/Sei ({{lang|ja|濟}}), one of the [[Five kings of Wa]], is supposed to be the {{lang|ja|王}} (King) which is appeared in the inscription {{lang|ja|王賜□□□敬□}} on the {{ill|Ōshimei iron sword|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文}} unearthed from the {{ill|Inaridai 1st Kofun|ja|稲荷台1号墳}} in [[Chiba prefecture]]. If this is the case, then Ingyō may not be {{lang|ja|大王}} (great king), but king.{{original research inline|date=November 2023}} As for King Wakatakeru ([[Emperor Yūryaku]]), who is identified{{by whom|date=November 2023}} with King Bu ({{lang|ja|武}}), there is the inscription {{lang|ja|獲加多支鹵大王}} on an iron sword unearthed in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] in [[Saitama prefecture]], as well as the inscription {{lang|ja|治天下獲□□□鹵大王}} on an iron sword unearthed in the [[Eta Funayama Kofun]] in [[Kumamoto prefecture]]. From these inscriptions, it is theorized{{by whom|date=November 2023}} that Yūryaku might have used the title "Chi Tenka Daiō" ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}) inside Japan. It also suggests the title "Chi Tenka Daiō" was first used in the late 5th century.
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the [[Nara period]], there are the scripts "{{lang|ja|大王、風姿…}}" (Daiō, fūshi...) in the first Enthronement record of Ohosazaki no Sumeramikoto ([[Emperor Nintoku]])<!--大鷦鷯天皇（おほさざきのすめらみこと）-->, but it is not certain that this ''Daiō'' had been used from the days of Emperor Nintoku, who ruled some 200 years before the compilation of the ''Nihon Shoki''. However, the expression of ''Daiō'' appears in the [[Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] record, and after that, it appears in the [[Emperor Ingyō|Ingyō]] record, [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] record, [[Emperor Kenzō|Kenzō]] record and [[Emperor Keitai|Keitai]] record, among others.
There is the inscription {{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 男弟王 在意紫沙加宮時 斯麻 念長寿 遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱 作此鏡」}} (according to Toshio Fukuyama) <ref>{{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 …… 作此鏡」}} can be read roughly: "''In the year of {{lang|ja|癸未}} (Kibi), August, Day 10, Great King (Daiō) year .... this Mirror is made ({{lang|ja|作此鏡}})''". </ref> on the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] (Portrait of person Mirror), owned by [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], [[Wakayama prefecture]], in which the words {{lang|ja|大王}} and {{lang|ja|男弟王}} appear. From this inscription, in the year ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' ({{lang|ja|癸未}}, ''Kibi'') <ref>See "[[Sexagenary cycle]]". ''Kibi (Mizunoto Hitsuji)'' is the name of year (year of "Water Goat"). There are 60 names of year according to Sexagenary cycle (''Kanshi''). Named year exists in each 60 years. See also [[:ja:%E7%99%B8%E6%9C%AA|ja:Mizunoto Hitsuji]] or [[:fr:Ch%C3%A8vre_d%27eau|French article]].</ref> when the mirror was made, the title ''Daiō'' is supposed to have been used. But there are various interpretations of what year this ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' year was. In several theories, it is: 383, 443, 503, or 623 AD. Among these years, 443 ([[Emperor Ingyō]]) or 503 ([[Emperor Buretsu]]) are considered most likely. If it is 443, the expression Daiō was used in around the age of Ingyō, in the middle 5th century. However, the characters in the inscription are difficult to interpret. In addition, the content of the inscription itself has various interpretations. The exact years when the expression ''Daiō'' started to be used are unclear.
<!-- skipped part exists HERE
--> In the [[Man'yōshū]], the expression {{lang|ja|大王}} is the most common, and 57 examples appear. Other expressions {{lang|ja|王}}, {{lang|ja|皇}}, {{lang|ja|大王}}, and {{lang|ja|大皇}} also appear. However, all these words are read "Ōkimi" or "Ohokimi", and there are no examples that the single character {{lang|ja|王}} is read "Kimi". The word {{lang|ja|大君}} is rarely used. There are only a few examples, such as the line {{lang|ja|やすみしし我が大君…}} (Yasumishi shi waga ōkimi...) by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]].<!-- omitted -- 万葉集には「大王」の表記が最も多く57例が見られ、他に「王」「皇」「大王」「大皇」が見られる。ただしいずれも“オオキミ”“オホキミ”と訓ませており、「王」を単一で“キミ”と訓む例はない。“キミ”に権威を背景とする君主を意味する「君」の字を当てた「大君」は万葉集には柿本人麻呂が詠んだ「やすみしし我が大君…」など数例が見えるのみである。橋本達雄は、記紀歌謡・万葉歌双方に見られるの表現を考察して、枕詞の「やすみしし」は全世界を統治する意で、天皇の称号と同様に道教の教理に基づいて案出されたものと説いている。--> <!--
There is a theory that [[Emperor Ingyō]], who is identified with {{lang|und|Sai or Sei|italics=no}} ({{lang|lzh|濟}}) of the ancient Japanese [[Five kings of Wa]], is the ''king'' in the inscription of the [[Inaridai Sword]], whose gift the sword is, according to its inscription: {{lang|lzh|王賜□□}}, ('[the] king bestows ..'). There is a theory that it was not the "Great King".{{explain|date=February 2022}}
In the case of King Wakatake ([[Emperor Yūryaku]], or Emperor Yusei), who is compared to [[Takeshi]], the inscription on [[Inariyama Sword]], an iron burial weapon excavated from the [[Inariyama Kofun]] in [[Saitama Prefecture]], reads, {{lang|und|Kari-Katashike Daio}}.{{meaning|date=February 2022}} The inscription on an iron sword excavated in the [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], from the [[Eta Funayama Kofun]] reads, {{lang|und|Osamu Tenka Kari}}{{lang|lzh|□□□□鹵大王}}. This suggests that the title {{lang|und|Ji Tenka Daioh}} was born around this time, the late 5th century.
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the Nara period (710-794), the title ''Daiō, fūshiki...'' appears in the period before the accession of Emperor Dōgi (''Emperor Nintoku''), but it is not clear whether the title was used from the time of Emperor Nintoku, long before its compilation. The word "daiō" first appears in the Ojinki, and then in the Yunkyōki, Yūryūki, Kensōki, and Jūtaiki.
The inscription on a "Personality Mirror", the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] in the collection of Sumida Hachiman Shrine in [[Wakayama Prefecture]] reads, "On the tenth day of the eighth month of the eighth year of the year of the King, when the King's younger brother was at the palace of Zishaga, he sent the two men of the Kaisaka Faijai to take the white upper and the same 200 droughts and made this mirror." (by Toshio Fukuyama). This mirror was made in the same way as the other mirrors. From this, it can be inferred that the title "Great King" was used in the year of the 60th year of the reign when the mirror was made, but there are several theories on the interpretation of the year of the 60th year of the reign, including AD 383, 443, 503, and 623. However, the exact date of use of the "Daewoong" inscription is not known. However, the exact date of use of the "Daewoong" inscription is not clear, as there are many characters that are not readily interpreted, including variants of the chronological The "Daewoong" inscription is not clear, as there are many characters that are not readily interpreted, including variants of the chronological
temporarily skipped
In the Sui Dynasty (581-618), the first mission to Sui China in the 20th year of the reign of Emperor Kai (600), described in the Sui Dynasty Book of Records as "{{Lang|zh|俀王姓阿毎字多利思北孤 號阿輩雞彌}}". The name of the king of China, Tarishibekko, is thought to have been "Ahakemi," which means "great god". In the second [[Japanese missions to Sui China]] of the 3rd year of the reign of Emperor Daigyo (607), the title "Son of Heaven" was used in the national epistle, "{{Lang|zh| 日出處天子致書日沒處天子無恙云}}". However, it is thought that the "Ookimi" (Great King) and the "Amae-no-shitashiro-shime-su Ookimi" (Great King) were used in Japan.
In the inscription on the halo of the statue of Yakushi Nyorai in the Golden Hall of [[Hōryū-ji]], built by [[Prince Shōtoku]] in the early [[7th century]], the names "Emperor Yōmei" (Great King), [[Emperor Suiko]], and the "Aeme-no-shitashiro-shime-su Ookimi" (Great King) were used in Japan, suggesting that the title "Ame-no-shita-shironoshime-no-oh" was used (Suiko dynasty theory). However, the inscription itself uses words such as "Emperor" and "Holy King of the Eastern Palace" as well as Japanese expressions such as {{lang|ja|Dai Goshimono Teshigaki}}, suggesting that it is too early to be from the Suiko period, and the style of the inscription has an early [[Tang dynasty]] feel to it. In addition to the theory of the Suiko dynasty, there is another theory that the statue was made in the Suiko period. In addition to the Suiko dynasty theory, the Tenchi dynasty theory and the Tembu and Jito dynasties theory are also known.
skipped
In the [[Man'yōshū]], "daiō" is the most common spelling, with 57 examples, and other spellings such as "o", "ō", "daiō", and "daiō" are also found. However, in all of these cases, the word is pronounced as "oh-kimi" or "oh-kimi," and there is no single example of the word "king" being pronounced as "kimi". There are only a few examples in the Man'yōshū of "Ookimi," in which the character for "kimi" is applied to the character for "kun," meaning a sovereign with a background of authority, such as in the poem "''Good night, my great sovereign...''" by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]] (Kakinomoto Maro). Hashimoto Tatsuo, examining the expressions found in both Kiki and Manyo poems, argues that the pillow word {{lang|und|yasumi-shi}} means 'to rule the whole world', and was conceived based on Taoist doctrine as well as the emperor's title.
--> <!-- errors so many hidden / corrections are needed
== Formation of "Emperor" == It is widely known that the notation '''[[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]'''' originates from the ''Emperor of Japan'', who is regarded as the ''supreme god of the universe'' in [[Taoism]] brought from the continent.
In the [[Emperor of Japan]], it is said to have been first published in the [[Nihon Shoki]] in September of the 16th year of the [[Empress Suiko|Suiko]] reign, "The Emperor of the East respectfully addresses the Emperor of the West. In addition, there is an article in the [[Tenjukoku Shūchō Mandala]]<ref>{{Cite journal|last=千田稔|year=2007|title=「天皇」号成立推古朝説の系譜 : もう一つの邪馬台国論争的状況|url=https://doi.org/10.15055/00000573|journal=日本研究|publisher=国際日本文化研究センター|volume=35|pages=405–419|doi=10.15055/00000573|issn=0915-0900|access-date=2021-09-28}}</ref>、And in the preface to [[Kaifūsō]], the emperor notation is used only for the period after [[Empress Jitō|Emperor Jitō]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=西野誠一|year=2005|title=天皇号の成立年代|url=https://hdl.handle.net/2297/1439|journal=金沢大学文学部日本史学研究室紀要|publisher=金沢大学文学部史学科日本史学研究室|issue=第1号|pages=21–32|issn=1880-2079|access-date=2021-09-28}}</ref> In recent years, the latter is the most likely explanation. In recent years, the latter theory is considered more likely.
The adoption of "emperor" as an official description of a sovereign was most likely in the [[Tenmu dynasty]]. It is speculated that [[Emperor Tenmu|Tenmu]] may have followed the example of [[Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Gaozong]] of the [[Tang dynasty]], who changed "emperor" to "emperor" in [[674]]. The name "Emperor" is the name of the supreme god of the universe in ancient China, [[Shangdi|tian emperor]], and is closely related to [[Taoism]].
Shavings of [[Mokkan]], in which characters such as "Otsu-ko", "Tsu-ko", and "Prince" can be seen, have been excavated from the Asuka-kyo site. These have been interpreted to refer to [[Prince Ōtsu|Prince Otsu]], the son of Tenmu, and are thought to date from the 10th year of Tenmu ([[681]]) based on other wooden strips excavated at the same time.The fact that the prince notation was used in the 10th year of the Tenmu Era is thought to be proof that the emperor notation was used before that.
In addition, "Sumeramikoto" and "Sumeroki" are used for the word "emperor. There was a theory that "Sumeru" was a corruption of "to rule", but this theory was rejected by [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]], and it is still unclear today.{{Efn|Another theory is that it is a corruption of the word "clear," which evokes sanctity from cleanliness, and that it is a corruption of the word "emperor," which expresses a shining and sparkling appearance.}}<ref name="kokugakuin2">{{Cite web |date=2014-04-29 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68627 |access-date=2022-04-01 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140429194813/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68627 |archive-date=29 April 2014 |url-status=dead}}</ref>。In the Manyoshu, there are twelve known instances of the word "emperor," of which seven are "Okimi" and five are "Sumeroki. From a comparison of the meanings of the two words, it is clear that "Okimi" is used for the emperor of the day, while "Sumeroki" is used for past [[List of emperors of Japan|the emperors of the past]] and the god of the emperors. It is known that "Okimi" is used for the current emperor and "Sumeroki" is used for past [[List of emperors of Japan]]<ref name="kokugakuin">{{Cite web |date=2016-03-05 |title=國學院デジタルミュージアム |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68630 |access-date=2022-04-01 |website= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305231304/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68630 |archive-date=5 March 2016 |url-status=dead}}</ref>。 -->

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}}{{Expand Japanese|大王 (ヤマト王権)|date=November 2023}}
'''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, ''Ōkimi'', ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''), was the title of the head of the [[Yamato Kingship]], or the [[monarch]] title of [[Wakoku]] (Old Japan). This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Asuka period]] in [[History of Japan|ancient Japan]]. <!-- {{Nihongo|Okimi|大王}} or {{Nihongo|Ame no shita shiroshimesu ōkimi|治天下大王}} is the title of [[Lord mayor]] of the [[Yamato Kingship]] from the [[Kofun period]] to the [[Asuka period]], or the title of [[Monarch]] of Japan. -->
== Etymology == The title "{{lang|ja|大王}}" (''Ōkimi'' or ''Daiō''), which is an [[Honorific|honorific title]] for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix "ō/oho," which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title ''kimi'' (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that ''Ōkimi'' is only an honorific form of ''kimi'', a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of [[Sino-Japanese vocabulary|kango]] (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）項目「大王」</ref> <!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Order]] began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. -->
== See also == * [[Suishō]] * [[Emperor of Japan]] * [[Kofun period]] * [[Asuka period]] * [[Five kings of Wa]]

If there are strange wording or other problems in this text, they are caused from the fact that the translator is not a native English user. You may correct those strange points, but you should have knowledge of the history of Japan. --> [[Category:Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Royal titles]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

'''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, ''Ōkimi'', ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''), was the title of the head of the [[Yamato Kingship]], or the [[monarch]] title of [[Wakoku]] (Old Japan). This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Asuka period]] in [[History of Japan|ancient Japan]]. <!-- {{Nihongo|Okimi|大王}} or {{Nihongo|Ame no shita shiroshimesu ōkimi|治天下大王}} is the title of [[Lord mayor]] of the [[Yamato Kingship]] from the [[Kofun period]] to the [[Asuka period]], or the title of [[Monarch]] of Japan. -->
== Etymology == The title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''Ōkimi'' or ''Daiō''), which is an [[Honorific|honorific title]] for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix ''ō'' or ''oho'', which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title ''kimi'' (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that ''Ōkimi'' is only an honorific form of ''kimi'', a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of [[Sino-Japanese vocabulary|kango]] (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）項目「大王」</ref> <!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Order]] began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. -->
== See also == * [[Suishō]] * [[Emperor of Japan]] * [[Kofun period]] * [[Asuka period]] * [[Five kings of Wa]]
== References == {{Reflist}} <!-- ''Notice:'' This text is the translation of the ja-article "[[:ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)|Ōkimi]]". This was translated by a human being, that means this is '''''not''' machine translation''. If there are strange wording or other problems in this text, they are caused from the fact that the translator is not a native English user. You may correct those strange points, but you should have knowledge of the history of Japan. --> [[Category:Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Royal titles]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

{{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}}{{Expand Japanese|大王 (ヤマト王権)|date=November 2023}}
'''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, also read as ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''), was the title of the head of the [[Yamato Kingship]], or the [[monarch]] title of [[Wakoku]] (Old Japan). This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Asuka period]] in [[History of Japan|ancient Japan]]. <!-- {{Nihongo|Okimi|大王}} or {{Nihongo|Ame no shita shiroshimesu ōkimi|治天下大王}} is the title of [[Lord mayor]] of the [[Yamato Kingship]] from the [[Kofun period]] to the [[Asuka period]], or the title of [[Monarch]] of Japan. -->
== Etymology == The title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''Ōkimi'' or ''Daiō''), which is an [[Honorific|honorific title]] for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix ''ō'' or ''oho'', which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title ''kimi'' (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that ''Ōkimi'' is only an honorific form of ''kimi'', a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of [[Sino-Japanese vocabulary|kango]] (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）項目「大王」</ref> <!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Order]] began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. -->

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}} {{Multiple issues| {{copyedit|date=February 2023}} }} '''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, also read as ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''),<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/天下-577513 Tenka (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 天下 [Tenka (the world under the heaven)]". It says that on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, there is the inscrption "「治天下[獲加多支鹵]大王」(あめのしたしろしめす[わかたけるの]おおきみ, Ame no shita shirosimesu [Wakatakeru no] Ōkimi). "Wakatakeru" is the name of the Ruler of Yamato (Wakoku), called Ōkimi (great king).</ref> was the title of the head of the [[Yamato Kingship]], or the [[monarch]] title of [[Wakoku]] (Old Japan).<ref name="nip-okimi">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The first is "the title for a King of [[Yamato kingship]]". "これらの大王は ....ヤマト政権の王の称号として用いられた".</ref> This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Asuka period]] in [[History of Japan|ancient Japan]].<ref name="nip-okimi" />
== Etymology == The title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''Ōkimi'' or ''Daiō''), which is an [[Honorific|honorific title]] for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix ''ō'' or ''oho'' which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title "kimi" (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that ''Ōkimi'' is only an honorific form of ''kimi'', a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of [[Sino-Japanese vocabulary|kango]] (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）項目「大王」</ref> There are several instances of the use of ''Ōkimi'', which is understood as a courtesy title of Emperor or royal family.<ref name="nip-okimi-2-3">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi 2,3 (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The second is the title of the substantial ruler of Japan, used in around 6th century. And the third is: A) after the establishment of the title "Tennō", {{lang|ja|大王}} was written like {{lang|ja|大君}} and was read as "ōkimi". B) Title of a member of the royal family during 2nd to 5th century. They were called "ōkimi" and written usually as {{lang|ja|王}} (male) or {{lang|ja|女王}} (female).</ref> <!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the [[Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Order]] began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999年）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. -->
== Title of ''King of Japan'' in Chinese documents == === ''King'' as a title in ancient China === The [[kanji]] title "{{lang|ja|王}}" (''Ō, Wang'') originally designated a Master of ''Chūgen'' ({{lang|ja|中原}}, [[Zhongyuan]]) in [[China proper|Inner China]]. In the [[Zhou dynasty]] period, {{lang|zh|王}} (''Wang'') was the title of the sole [[Son of Heaven]] who rules the [[Tianxia]]. However, some great powers in the region of the [[Yangtze civilization]] did not want to stand in subordinate positions of the nations of the [[Yellow River civilization]] in [[North China]], such as [[Chu (state)|Chu]], [[Wu (state)|Wu]] and [[Yue (state)|Yue]]. Some of their monarchs titled themselves ''Wang''.
When China entered into the [[Warring States period]], the monarchs of the great nations among the [[Ancient Chinese states|states of North China]] who were originally subjects of the Zhou King, but achieved territorial statehood, called themselves sole ''Wang'' of the Tianxia in place of the Zhou King. Thus, there were numerous claims to the throne in mainland China. Thereafter, Ying Zheng ({{lang|zh|嬴政}}), the King of [[Qin (state)|Qin]] (Emperor [[Qin Shi Huang|Shi Huang]]), who unified China for the first time in 221 BC, adopted the title "Emperor" ({{lang|zh|皇帝}}, ''Huángdì'') instead of title "King", which had been degraded. The King title ''Wang'' became the title granted to subjects of the Emperor, or that assigned to heads of neighboring states who recognized the authority of the Qin Emperor as Master of Tianxia, with a connotation of a subordinate rank. The latter usage was established thereafter. The monarch of [[Xiongnu]] stood on even ground with the Emperor of Qin, therefore his title was [[Chanyu]], not ''Wang''.
=== Early title of ''King of Japan'' === The first appearance of King title related to old Japan is {{lang|ja|漢委奴国王}} (''Kan no Wa no Na no kokuō'', King of Na in Wa of [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Han]]) engraved on the [[King of Na gold seal|gold seal]] which was bestowed on the king of [[Nakoku]] by the emperor [[Emperor Guangwu of Han|Guangwu]] of Han in 57 AD.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/漢倭奴国王印-471192 Wa-no-Na-no-Kokuo-In (Kotobank)] See "デジタル大辞泉 -「漢倭奴国王印」". It says "印文は「漢委奴国王」の5字が刻まれている (the inscrption on the seal is 5 letters: Kan Wa Na Koku O)". The seal (and ribbon) were bestowed on king of Nakoku by the emperor Guangwu (光武帝) in 57 AD.</ref>
The word ''Wakoku ō'' (King of Wa) appears in the article dated to the first year of ''Eisho'' (107 AD), in the record of [[Emperor An of Han|Emperor An]] in the ''[[Book of the Later Han]]''. The full title, as written in the record of emperor An, is: "[[Suishō]], the king of Wa, and other". If "Wakoku ō" refers to the King of [[Wakoku]] as a head of the states union, other than a head of small regional state, this description shows the establishment of the [[Wakoku]].<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/帥升-1083317 Suisho (Kotobank)] See "山川 日本史小辞典 改訂新版 -「帥升」". It says Suishō is the king of Wakoku from the late 1st cenrury to the early 2nd century (1世紀末～2世紀初めの倭国の王).</ref>
In addition, [[Himiko]] (c. 180 AD–c. 247 AD) was authorised as the unified Queen of Wakoku (whose capital was [[Yamatai|Yamatai koku]]) by the [[Cao Wei|Wei]] dynasty. There is a theory that the government that existed during Himiko's rule was an early form of the [[Yamato Kingship]], though this has been disputed.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/卑弥呼-120826 Himiko (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) -「卑弥呼」", "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「卑弥呼」" and "ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 -「卑弥呼」".</ref>
== Establishment of the ''Daiō (Great King)'' expression == The kanji letters of the title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''ōkimi'', great king) was first appeared in the inscription of the iron sword unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[Saitama prefecture]].<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "百科事典マイペディア 「大王（おおきみ）」".</ref>
On the other hand, on the iron sword which is a silver inlaid sword, unearthed from the Eta Funayama kofun, [[Kumamoto prefecture]], there was the inscription. It was difficult to read this inscription. In 1934, Toshio Fukuyama deciphered the inscription and he read it partially as {{lang|ja|「治天下𤟱□□□歯大王世□□」}}. From this reading, Fukuyama identified that ''Daiō'' ({{lang|ja|大王}}) in this script is the [[Emperor Hansho]]. But in 1978, on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[Saitama prefecture]], the inscription was discovered. When Toshio Kishi and other researchers tried to decipher this inscription, they re-examined the reading of the script on the sword from the Eta Funayama kofun. They found the script can be read as {{lang|ja|「治天下獲加多支鹵大王」}}. {{lang|ja|「獲加多支鹵」}} is read "Wakatakeru", and it is the Japanese name of the [[Emperor Yuryaku]]. This ''daiō'' inscribed on the sword might be Yuryaku, and this is the first used example of ''Chi Tenka Daiō'', in the late 5th century.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/江田船山古墳-36675 Eta Funayama kofun (Kotobank)] See "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「江田船山古墳」"</ref>
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the [[Nara period]], there are the scripts "{{lang|ja|大王、風姿…}}" (''Daiō, fūshi''...) in the first Enthronement record of Ohosazaki no Sumeramikoto ([[Emperor Nintoku]])<!--大鷦鷯天皇（おほさざきのすめらみこと）-->, but it is not certain that this ''Daiō'' had been used from the days of Emperor Nintoku, who ruled some 200 years before the compilation of the ''Nihon Shoki''. However, the expression of ''Daiō'' appears in the [[Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] record, and after that, it appears in the [[Emperor Ingyō|Ingyō]] record, [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] record, [[Emperor Kenzō|Kenzō]] record and [[Emperor Keitai|Keitai]] record, among others.
There is the inscription {{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 男弟王 在意紫沙加宮時 斯麻 念長寿 遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱 作此鏡」}} (according to Toshio Fukuyama) <ref>{{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 …… 作此鏡」}} can be read roughly: "''In the year of {{lang|ja|癸未}} (Kibi), August, Day 10, Great King (Daiō) year .... this Mirror is made ({{lang|ja|作此鏡}})''". </ref> on the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] (Portrait of person Mirror), owned by [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], [[Wakayama prefecture]], in which the words {{lang|ja|大王}} and {{lang|ja|男弟王}} appear. From this inscription, in the year ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' ({{lang|ja|癸未}}, ''Kibi'') <ref>See "[[Sexagenary cycle]]". ''Kibi (Mizunoto Hitsuji)'' is the name of year (year of "Water Goat"). There are 60 names of year according to Sexagenary cycle (''Kanshi''). Named year exists in each 60 years. See also [[:ja:%E7%99%B8%E6%9C%AA|ja:Mizunoto Hitsuji]] or [[:fr:Ch%C3%A8vre_d%27eau|French article]].</ref> when the mirror was made, the title ''Daiō'' is supposed to have been used. But there are various interpretations of what year this ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' year was. In several theories, it is: 383, 443, 503, or 623 AD. Among these years, 443 ([[Emperor Ingyō]]) or 503 ([[Emperor Buretsu]]) are considered most likely. If it is 443, the expression Daiō was used in around the age of Ingyō, in the middle 5th century. However, the characters in the inscription are difficult to interpret. In addition, the content of the inscription itself has various interpretations. The exact years when the expression ''Daiō'' started to be used are unclear. <!-- Now unsourced In the [[Man'yōshū]], the expression {{lang|ja|大王}} is the most common, and 57 examples appear. Other expressions {{lang|ja|王}}, {{lang|ja|皇}}, {{lang|ja|大王}}, and {{lang|ja|大皇}} also appear. However, all these words are read ''Ōkimi'' or ''Ohokimi'', and there are no examples that the single character {{lang|ja|王}} is read "Kimi". The word {{lang|ja|大君}} is rarely used. There are only a few examples, such as the line {{lang|ja|やすみしし我が大君…}} (Yasumishi shi waga ōkimi...) by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]].--><!-- omitted -- 万葉集には「大王」の表記が最も多く57例が見られ、他に「王」「皇」「大王」「大皇」が見られる。ただしいずれも“オオキミ”“オホキミ”と訓ませており、「王」を単一で“キミ”と訓む例はない。“キミ”に権威を背景とする君主を意味する「君」の字を当てた「大君」は万葉集には柿本人麻呂が詠んだ「やすみしし我が大君…」など数例が見えるのみである。橋本達雄は、記紀歌謡・万葉歌双方に見られるの表現を考察して、枕詞の「やすみしし」は全世界を統治する意で、天皇の称号と同様に道教の教理に基づいて案出されたものと説いている。-->

You may correct those strange points, but you should have knowledge of the history of Japan. --> [[Category:Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Royal titles]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

When China entered into the [[Warring States period]], the monarchs of the great nations among the [[Ancient Chinese states|states of North China]] who were originally subjects of the Zhou King, but achieved territorial statehood, called themselves sole ''Wang'' of the Tianxia in place of the Zhou King. Thus, there were numerous claims to the throne in mainland China. Thereafter, Ying Zheng ({{lang|zh|嬴政}}), the King of [[Qin (state)|Qin]] (Emperor [[Qin Shi Huang|Shi Huang]]), who unified China for the first time in 221 BC, adopted the title "Emperor" ({{lang|zh|皇帝}}, ''Huángdì'') instead of title "King", which had been degraded. The King title ''Wang'' became the title granted to subjects of the Emperor, or that assigned to heads of neighboring states who recognized the authority of the Qin Emperor as Master of Tianxia, with a connotation of a subordinate rank. The latter usage was established thereafter. The monarch of [[Xiongnu]] stood on even ground with the Emperor of Qin, therefore his title was [[Chanyu]], not ''Wang''.
=== Early title of ''King of Japan'' === The first appearance of King title related to old Japan is {{lang|ja|漢委奴国王}} (''Kan no Wa no Na no kokuō'', King of Na in Wa of [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Han]]) engraved on the [[King of Na gold seal|gold seal]] which was bestowed on the king of [[Nakoku]] by the emperor [[Emperor Guangwu of Han|Guangwu]] of Han in 57 AD.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/漢倭奴国王印-471192 Wa-no-Na-no-Kokuo-In (Kotobank)] See "デジタル大辞泉 -「漢倭奴国王印」". It says "印文は「漢委奴国王」の5字が刻まれている (the inscrption on the seal is 5 letters: Kan Wa Na Koku O)". The seal (and ribbon) were bestowed on king of Nakoku by the emperor Guangwu (光武帝) in 57 AD.</ref>
The word ''Wakoku ō'' (King of Wa) appears in the article dated to the first year of ''Eisho'' (107 AD), in the record of [[Emperor An of Han|Emperor An]] in the ''[[Book of the Later Han]]''. The full title, as written in the record of emperor An, is: "[[Suishō]], the king of Wa, and other". If "Wakoku ō" refers to the King of [[Wakoku]] as a head of the states union, other than a head of small regional state, this description shows the establishment of the [[Wakoku]].<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/帥升-1083317 Suisho (Kotobank)] See "山川 日本史小辞典 改訂新版 -「帥升」". It says Suishō is the king of Wakoku from the late 1st cenrury to the early 2nd century (1世紀末～2世紀初めの倭国の王).</ref>
In addition, [[Himiko]] (c. 180 AD–c. 247 AD) was authorised as the unified Queen of Wakoku (whose capital was [[Yamatai|Yamatai koku]]) by the [[Cao Wei|Wei]] dynasty. There is a theory that the government that existed during Himiko's rule was an early form of the [[Yamato Kingship]], though this has been disputed.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/卑弥呼-120826 Himiko (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) -「卑弥呼」", "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「卑弥呼」" and "ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 -「卑弥呼」".</ref>
== Establishment of the ''Daiō (Great King)'' expression == The kanji letters of the title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''ōkimi'', great king) was first appeared in the inscription of the iron sword unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[Saitama prefecture]].<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "百科事典マイペディア 「大王（おおきみ）」".</ref>
On the other hand, on the iron sword which is a silver inlaid sword, unearthed from the Eta Funayama kofun, [[Kumamoto prefecture]], there was the inscription. It was difficult to read this inscription. In 1934, Toshio Fukuyama deciphered the inscription and he read it partially as {{lang|ja|「治天下𤟱□□□歯大王世□□」}}. From this reading, Fukuyama identified that ''Daiō'' ({{lang|ja|大王}}) in this script is the [[Emperor Hansho]]. But in 1978, on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[Saitama prefecture]], the inscription was discovered. When Toshio Kishi and other researchers tried to decipher this inscription, they re-examined the reading of the script on the sword from the Eta Funayama kofun. They found the script can be read as {{lang|ja|「治天下獲加多支鹵大王」}}. {{lang|ja|「獲加多支鹵」}} is read "Wakatakeru", and it is the Japanese name of the [[Emperor Yuryaku]]. This ''daiō'' inscribed on the sword might be Yuryaku, and this is the first used example of ''Chi Tenka Daiō'', in the late 5th century.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/江田船山古墳-36675 Eta Funayama kofun (Kotobank)] See "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「江田船山古墳」"</ref>
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the [[Nara period]], there are the scripts "{{lang|ja|大王、風姿…}}" (''Daiō, fūshi''...) in the first Enthronement record of Ohosazaki no Sumeramikoto ([[Emperor Nintoku]])<!--大鷦鷯天皇（おほさざきのすめらみこと）-->, but it is not certain that this ''Daiō'' had been used from the days of Emperor Nintoku, who ruled some 200 years before the compilation of the ''Nihon Shoki''. However, the expression of ''Daiō'' appears in the [[Emperor Ōjin|Ōjin]] record, and after that, it appears in the [[Emperor Ingyō|Ingyō]] record, [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] record, [[Emperor Kenzō|Kenzō]] record and [[Emperor Keitai|Keitai]] record, among others.
There is the inscription {{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 男弟王 在意紫沙加宮時 斯麻 念長寿 遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱 作此鏡」}} (according to Toshio Fukuyama) <ref>{{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 …… 作此鏡」}} can be read roughly: "''In the year of {{lang|ja|癸未}} (Kibi), August, Day 10, Great King (Daiō) year .... this Mirror is made ({{lang|ja|作此鏡}})''". </ref> on the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] (Portrait of person Mirror), owned by [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], [[Wakayama prefecture]], in which the words {{lang|ja|大王}} and {{lang|ja|男弟王}} appear. From this inscription, in the year ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' ({{lang|ja|癸未}}, ''Kibi'') <ref>See "[[Sexagenary cycle]]". ''Kibi (Mizunoto Hitsuji)'' is the name of year (year of "Water Goat"). There are 60 names of year according to Sexagenary cycle (''Kanshi''). Named year exists in each 60 years. See also [[:ja:%E7%99%B8%E6%9C%AA|ja:Mizunoto Hitsuji]] or [[:fr:Ch%C3%A8vre_d%27eau|French article]].</ref> when the mirror was made, the title ''Daiō'' is supposed to have been used. But there are various interpretations of what year this ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' year was. In several theories, it is: 383, 443, 503, or 623 AD. Among these years, 443 ([[Emperor Ingyō]]) or 503 ([[Emperor Buretsu]]) are considered most likely. If it is 443, the expression Daiō was used in around the age of Ingyō, in the middle 5th century. However, the characters in the inscription are difficult to interpret. In addition, the content of the inscription itself has various interpretations. The exact years when the expression ''Daiō'' started to be used are unclear. <!-- Now unsourced In the [[Man'yōshū]], the expression {{lang|ja|大王}} is the most common, and 57 examples appear. Other expressions {{lang|ja|王}}, {{lang|ja|皇}}, {{lang|ja|大王}}, and {{lang|ja|大皇}} also appear. However, all these words are read ''Ōkimi'' or ''Ohokimi'', and there are no examples that the single character {{lang|ja|王}} is read "Kimi". The word {{lang|ja|大君}} is rarely used. There are only a few examples, such as the line {{lang|ja|やすみしし我が大君…}} (Yasumishi shi waga ōkimi...) by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]].--><!-- omitted -- 万葉集には「大王」の表記が最も多く57例が見られ、他に「王」「皇」「大王」「大皇」が見られる。ただしいずれも“オオキミ”“オホキミ”と訓ませており、「王」を単一で“キミ”と訓む例はない。“キミ”に権威を背景とする君主を意味する「君」の字を当てた「大君」は万葉集には柿本人麻呂が詠んだ「やすみしし我が大君…」など数例が見えるのみである。橋本達雄は、記紀歌謡・万葉歌双方に見られるの表現を考察して、枕詞の「やすみしし」は全世界を統治する意で、天皇の称号と同様に道教の教理に基づいて案出されたものと説いている。-->

This was translated by a human being, that means this is '''''not''' machine translation''. You may correct those strange points, but you should have knowledge of the history of Japan. --> [[Category:Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Royal titles]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]
