<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]]
{{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}}
'''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, also read as ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''),<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/天下-577513 Tenka (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 天下 [Tenka (the world under the heaven)]". It says that on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, there is the inscrption "「治天下[獲加多支鹵]大王」(あめのしたしろしめす[わかたけるの]おおきみ, Ame no shita shirosimesu [Wakatakeru no] Ōkimi). "Wakatakeru" is the name of the Ruler of Yamato (Wakoku), called Ōkimi (great king).</ref> was the title of the head of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}, or the {{ill|Monarch|en|Monarch|WD=Q116|lt=Monarch}} title of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku}} (Old Japan).<ref name="nip-okimi">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The first is "the title for a King of {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}". "これらの大王は ....ヤマト政権の王の称号として用いられた".</ref> This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} in {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=ancient Japan}}.<ref name="nip-okimi" />
== Etymology == The title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''Ōkimi'' or ''Daiō''), which is an {{ill|Honorific|en|Honorific|WD=Q1326966|lt=honorific title}} for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[:en:Kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix ''ō'' or ''oho'' which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title "kimi" (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that ''Ōkimi'' is only an honorific form of ''kimi'', a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of {{ill|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|en|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|WD=Q4222379|lt=kango}} (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999th year ）項目「大王」</ref> There are several instances of the use of ''Ōkimi'', which is understood as a courtesy title of Emperor or royal family.<ref name="nip-okimi-2-3">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi 2,3 (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The second is the title of the substantial ruler of Japan, used in around 6th century. And the third is: A) after the establishment of the title "Tennō", {{lang|ja|大王}} was written like {{lang|ja|大君}} and was read as "ōkimi". B) Title of a member of the royal family during 2nd to 5th century. They were called "ōkimi" and written usually as {{lang|ja|王}} (male) or {{lang|ja|女王}} (female).</ref>
== Title of ''King of Japan'' in Chinese documents == === ''King'' as a title in ancient China === The {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}} title "{{lang|ja|王}}" (''Ō, Wang'') originally designated a Master of ''Chūgen'' ({{lang|ja|中原}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan|en|Zhongyuan|WD=Q190277|lt=Zhongyuan}}) in {{ill|China proper|en|China proper|WD=Q2079794|lt=Inner China}}. In the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty}} period, {{lang|zh|王}} (''Wang'') was the title of the sole {{ill|Son of Heaven|en|Son of Heaven|WD=Q1147432|lt=Son of Heaven}} who rules the {{ill|Tianxia|en|Tianxia|WD=Q955223|lt=Tianxia}}. However, some great powers in the region of the {{ill|Yangtze civilization|en|Yangtze civilization|WD=Q856310|lt=Yangtze civilization}} did not want to stand in subordinate positions of the nations of the {{ill|Yellow River civilization|en|Yellow River civilization|WD=Q28584|lt=Yellow River civilization}} in {{ill|North China|en|North China|WD=Q1046848|lt=North China}}, such as {{ill|Chu (state)|en|Chu (state)|WD=Q504759|lt=Chu}}, {{ill|Wu (state)|en|Wu (state)|WD=Q912068|lt=Wu}} and {{ill|Yue (state)|en|Yue (state)|WD=Q1137403|lt=Yue}}. Some of their monarchs titled themselves ''Wang''.
When China entered into the {{ill|Warring States period|en|Warring States period|WD=Q185063|lt=Warring States period}}, the monarchs of the great nations among the {{ill|Ancient Chinese states|en|Ancient Chinese states|WD=Q836688|lt=states of North China}} who were originally subjects of the Zhou King, but achieved territorial statehood, called themselves sole ''Wang'' of the Tianxia in place of the Zhou King. Thus, there were numerous claims to the throne in mainland China. Thereafter, Ying Zheng ({{lang|zh|嬴政}}), the King of [[Qin (state)|Qin]] (Emperor {{ill|Qin Shi Huang|en|Qin Shi Huang|WD=Q7192|lt=Shi Huang}}), who unified China for the first time in 221 BC, adopted the title "Emperor" ({{lang|zh|皇帝}}, ''Huángdì'') instead of title "King", which had been degraded. The King title ''Wang'' became the title granted to subjects of the Emperor, or that assigned to heads of neighboring states who recognized the authority of the Qin Emperor as Master of Tianxia, with a connotation of a subordinate Rank. The latter usage was established thereafter. The monarch of {{ill|Xiongnu|en|Xiongnu|WD=Q188836|lt=Xiongnu}} stood on even ground with the Emperor of Qin, therefore his title was {{ill|Chanyu|en|Chanyu|WD=Q1062502|lt=Chanyu}}, not ''Wang''.
=== Early title of ''King of Japan'' === The first appearance of King title related to old Japan is {{lang|ja|漢委奴国王}} (''Kan no Wa no Na no kokuō'', King of Na in Wa of [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Han]]) engraved on the [[King of Na gold seal|gold seal]] which was bestowed on the king of {{ill|Nakoku|en|Nakoku|WD=Q600473|lt=Nakoku}} by the emperor {{ill|Emperor Guangwu of Han|en|Emperor Guangwu of Han|WD=Q7268|lt=Guangwu}} of Han in 57 AD.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/漢倭奴国王印-471192 Wa-no-Na-no-Kokuo-In (Kotobank)] See "デジタル大辞泉 -「漢倭奴国王印」". It says "印文は「漢委奴国王」の5字が刻まれている (the inscrption on the seal is 5 letters: Kan Wa Na Koku O)". The seal (and ribbon) were bestowed on king of Nakoku by the emperor Guangwu (光武帝) in 57 AD.</ref>
The word ''Wakoku ō'' (King of Wa) appears in the article dated to the first year of ''Eisho'' (107 AD), in the record of {{ill|Emperor An of Han|en|Emperor An of Han|WD=Q7285|lt=Emperor An}} in the ''{{ill|Book of the Later Han|en|Book of the Later Han|WD=Q430428|lt=Book of the Later Han}}''. The full title, as written in the record of emperor An, is: "{{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō}}, the king of Wa, and other". If "Wakoku ō" refers to the King of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku}} as a head of the states union, other than a head of small regional state, this description shows the establishment of the {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/帥升-1083317 Suisho (Kotobank)] See "山川 日本史小辞典 改訂新版 -「帥升」". It says Suishō is the king of Wakoku from the late 1st cenrury to the early 2nd century (1世紀末～2世紀初めの倭国の王).</ref>
In addition, [[Himiko]] (c. 180 AD–c. 247 AD) was authorised as the unified Queen of Wakoku (whose capital was {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai koku}}) by the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei}} dynasty. There is a theory that the government that existed during Himiko's rule was an early form of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}, though this has been disputed.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/卑弥呼-120826 Himiko (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) -「卑弥呼」", "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「卑弥呼」" and "ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 -「卑弥呼」".</ref>
== Establishment of the ''Daiō (Great King)'' expression == The kanji letters of the title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''ōkimi'', great king) was first appeared in the inscription of the iron sword unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]].<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "百科事典マイペディア 「大王（おおきみ）」".</ref>
On the other hand, on the iron sword which is a silver inlaid sword, unearthed from the Eta Funayama kofun, [[:en:Kumamoto prefecture|Kumamoto prefecture]], there was the inscription. It was difficult to read this inscription. In 1934, Toshio Fukuyama deciphered the inscription and he read it partially as {{lang|ja|「治天下𤟱□□□歯大王世□□」}}. From this reading, Fukuyama identified that ''Daiō'' ({{lang|ja|大王}}) in this script is the [[:en:Emperor Hansho|Emperor Hansho]]. But in 1978, on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]], the inscription was discovered. When Toshio Kishi and other researchers tried to decipher this inscription, they re-examined the reading of the script on the sword from the Eta Funayama kofun. They found the script can be read as {{lang|ja|「治天下獲加多支鹵大王」}}. {{lang|ja|「獲加多支鹵」}} is read "Wakatakeru", and it is the Japanese name of the [[:en:Emperor Yuryaku|Emperor Yuryaku]]. This ''daiō'' inscribed on the sword might be Yuryaku, and this is the first used example of ''Chi Tenka Daiō'', in the late 5th century.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/江田船山古墳-36675 Eta Funayama kofun (Kotobank)] See "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「江田船山古墳」"</ref>
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, there are the scripts "{{lang|ja|大王、風姿…}}" (''Daiō, fūshi''...) in the first Enthronement record of Ohosazaki no Sumeramikoto ({{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku}}), but it is not certain that this ''Daiō'' had been used from the days of Emperor Nintoku, who ruled some 200 years before the compilation of the ''Nihon Shoki''. However, the expression of ''Daiō'' appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Ōjin}} record, and after that, it appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Ingyō}} record, {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku}} record, {{ill|Emperor Kenzō|en|Emperor Kenzō|WD=Q360966|lt=Kenzō}} record and {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Keitai}} record, among others.
There is the inscription {{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 男弟王 在意紫沙加宮時 斯麻 念長寿 遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱 作此鏡」}} (according to Toshio Fukuyama) <ref>{{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 …… 作此鏡」}} can be read roughly: "''In the year of {{lang|ja|癸未}} (Kibi), August, Day 10, Great King (Daiō) year .... this Mirror is made ({{lang|ja|作此鏡}})''". </ref> on the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] (Portrait of person Mirror), owned by [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Wakayama prefecture|Wakayama prefecture]], in which the words {{lang|ja|大王}} and {{lang|ja|男弟王}} appear. From this inscription, in the year ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' ({{lang|ja|癸未}}, ''Kibi'') <ref>See "{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Sexagenary cycle}}". ''Kibi (Mizunoto Hitsuji)'' is the name of year (year of "Water Goat"). There are 60 names of year according to Sexagenary cycle (''Kanshi''). Named year exists in each 60 years. See also [[:ja:%E7%99%B8%E6%9C%AA|ja:Mizunoto Hitsuji]] or [[:fr:Ch%C3%A8vre_d%27eau|French article]].</ref> when the mirror was made, the title ''Daiō'' is supposed to have been used. But there are various interpretations of what year this ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' year was. In several theories, it is: 383, 443, 503, or 623 AD. Among these years, 443 ({{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō}}) or 503 ({{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu}}) are considered most likely. If it is 443, the expression Daiō was used in around the age of Ingyō, in the middle 5th century. However, the characters in the inscription are difficult to interpret. In addition, the content of the inscription itself has various interpretations. The exact years when the expression ''Daiō'' started to be used are unclear.
== See also == * {{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō}} * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}} * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} * [[Five kings of Wa]]

In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. {{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}}
'''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, also read as ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''),<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/天下-577513 Tenka (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 天下 [Tenka (the world under the heaven)]". It says that on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, there is the inscrption "「治天下[獲加多支鹵]大王」(あめのしたしろしめす[わかたけるの]おおきみ, Ame no shita shirosimesu [Wakatakeru no] Ōkimi). "Wakatakeru" is the name of the Ruler of Yamato (Wakoku), called Ōkimi (great king).</ref> was the title of the head of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}, or the {{ill|Monarch|en|Monarch|WD=Q116|lt=Monarch}} title of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku}} (Old Japan).<ref name="nip-okimi">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The first is "the title for a King of {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}". "これらの大王は ....ヤマト政権の王の称号として用いられた".</ref> This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} in {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=ancient Japan}}.<ref name="nip-okimi" />
== Etymology == The title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''Ōkimi'' or ''Daiō''), which is an {{ill|Honorific|en|Honorific|WD=Q1326966|lt=honorific title}} for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[:en:Kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix ''ō'' or ''oho'' which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title "kimi" (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that ''Ōkimi'' is only an honorific form of ''kimi'', a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of {{ill|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|en|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|WD=Q4222379|lt=kango}} (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999th year ）項目「大王」</ref> There are several instances of the use of ''Ōkimi'', which is understood as a courtesy title of Emperor or royal family.<ref name="nip-okimi-2-3">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi 2,3 (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The second is the title of the substantial ruler of Japan, used in around 6th century. And the third is: A) after the establishment of the title "Tennō", {{lang|ja|大王}} was written like {{lang|ja|大君}} and was read as "ōkimi". B) Title of a member of the royal family during 2nd to 5th century. They were called "ōkimi" and written usually as {{lang|ja|王}} (male) or {{lang|ja|女王}} (female).</ref>
== Title of ''King of Japan'' in Chinese documents == === ''King'' as a title in ancient China === The {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}} title "{{lang|ja|王}}" (''Ō, Wang'') originally designated a Master of ''Chūgen'' ({{lang|ja|中原}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan|en|Zhongyuan|WD=Q190277|lt=Zhongyuan}}) in {{ill|China proper|en|China proper|WD=Q2079794|lt=Inner China}}. In the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty}} period, {{lang|zh|王}} (''Wang'') was the title of the sole {{ill|Son of Heaven|en|Son of Heaven|WD=Q1147432|lt=Son of Heaven}} who rules the {{ill|Tianxia|en|Tianxia|WD=Q955223|lt=Tianxia}}. However, some great powers in the region of the {{ill|Yangtze civilization|en|Yangtze civilization|WD=Q856310|lt=Yangtze civilization}} did not want to stand in subordinate positions of the nations of the {{ill|Yellow River civilization|en|Yellow River civilization|WD=Q28584|lt=Yellow River civilization}} in {{ill|North China|en|North China|WD=Q1046848|lt=North China}}, such as {{ill|Chu (state)|en|Chu (state)|WD=Q504759|lt=Chu}}, {{ill|Wu (state)|en|Wu (state)|WD=Q912068|lt=Wu}} and {{ill|Yue (state)|en|Yue (state)|WD=Q1137403|lt=Yue}}. Some of their monarchs titled themselves ''Wang''.
When China entered into the {{ill|Warring States period|en|Warring States period|WD=Q185063|lt=Warring States period}}, the monarchs of the great nations among the {{ill|Ancient Chinese states|en|Ancient Chinese states|WD=Q836688|lt=states of North China}} who were originally subjects of the Zhou King, but achieved territorial statehood, called themselves sole ''Wang'' of the Tianxia in place of the Zhou King. Thus, there were numerous claims to the throne in mainland China. Thereafter, Ying Zheng ({{lang|zh|嬴政}}), the King of [[Qin (state)|Qin]] (Emperor {{ill|Qin Shi Huang|en|Qin Shi Huang|WD=Q7192|lt=Shi Huang}}), who unified China for the first time in 221 BC, adopted the title "Emperor" ({{lang|zh|皇帝}}, ''Huángdì'') instead of title "King", which had been degraded. The King title ''Wang'' became the title granted to subjects of the Emperor, or that assigned to heads of neighboring states who recognized the authority of the Qin Emperor as Master of Tianxia, with a connotation of a subordinate Rank. The latter usage was established thereafter. The monarch of {{ill|Xiongnu|en|Xiongnu|WD=Q188836|lt=Xiongnu}} stood on even ground with the Emperor of Qin, therefore his title was {{ill|Chanyu|en|Chanyu|WD=Q1062502|lt=Chanyu}}, not ''Wang''.
=== Early title of ''King of Japan'' === The first appearance of King title related to old Japan is {{lang|ja|漢委奴国王}} (''Kan no Wa no Na no kokuō'', King of Na in Wa of [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Han]]) engraved on the [[King of Na gold seal|gold seal]] which was bestowed on the king of {{ill|Nakoku|en|Nakoku|WD=Q600473|lt=Nakoku}} by the emperor {{ill|Emperor Guangwu of Han|en|Emperor Guangwu of Han|WD=Q7268|lt=Guangwu}} of Han in 57 AD.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/漢倭奴国王印-471192 Wa-no-Na-no-Kokuo-In (Kotobank)] See "デジタル大辞泉 -「漢倭奴国王印」". It says "印文は「漢委奴国王」の5字が刻まれている (the inscrption on the seal is 5 letters: Kan Wa Na Koku O)". The seal (and ribbon) were bestowed on king of Nakoku by the emperor Guangwu (光武帝) in 57 AD.</ref>
The word ''Wakoku ō'' (King of Wa) appears in the article dated to the first year of ''Eisho'' (107 AD), in the record of {{ill|Emperor An of Han|en|Emperor An of Han|WD=Q7285|lt=Emperor An}} in the ''{{ill|Book of the Later Han|en|Book of the Later Han|WD=Q430428|lt=Book of the Later Han}}''. The full title, as written in the record of emperor An, is: "{{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō}}, the king of Wa, and other". If "Wakoku ō" refers to the King of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku}} as a head of the states union, other than a head of small regional state, this description shows the establishment of the {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/帥升-1083317 Suisho (Kotobank)] See "山川 日本史小辞典 改訂新版 -「帥升」". It says Suishō is the king of Wakoku from the late 1st cenrury to the early 2nd century (1世紀末～2世紀初めの倭国の王).</ref>
In addition, [[Himiko]] (c. 180 AD–c. 247 AD) was authorised as the unified Queen of Wakoku (whose capital was {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai koku}}) by the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei}} dynasty. There is a theory that the government that existed during Himiko's rule was an early form of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship}}, though this has been disputed.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/卑弥呼-120826 Himiko (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) -「卑弥呼」", "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「卑弥呼」" and "ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 -「卑弥呼」".</ref>
== Establishment of the ''Daiō (Great King)'' expression == The kanji letters of the title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''ōkimi'', great king) was first appeared in the inscription of the iron sword unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]].<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "百科事典マイペディア 「大王（おおきみ）」".</ref>
On the other hand, on the iron sword which is a silver inlaid sword, unearthed from the Eta Funayama kofun, [[:en:Kumamoto prefecture|Kumamoto prefecture]], there was the inscription. It was difficult to read this inscription. In 1934, Toshio Fukuyama deciphered the inscription and he read it partially as {{lang|ja|「治天下𤟱□□□歯大王世□□」}}. From this reading, Fukuyama identified that ''Daiō'' ({{lang|ja|大王}}) in this script is the [[:en:Emperor Hansho|Emperor Hansho]]. But in 1978, on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]], the inscription was discovered. When Toshio Kishi and other researchers tried to decipher this inscription, they re-examined the reading of the script on the sword from the Eta Funayama kofun. They found the script can be read as {{lang|ja|「治天下獲加多支鹵大王」}}. {{lang|ja|「獲加多支鹵」}} is read "Wakatakeru", and it is the Japanese name of the [[:en:Emperor Yuryaku|Emperor Yuryaku]]. This ''daiō'' inscribed on the sword might be Yuryaku, and this is the first used example of ''Chi Tenka Daiō'', in the late 5th century.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/江田船山古墳-36675 Eta Funayama kofun (Kotobank)] See "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「江田船山古墳」"</ref>
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, there are the scripts "{{lang|ja|大王、風姿…}}" (''Daiō, fūshi''...) in the first Enthronement record of Ohosazaki no Sumeramikoto ({{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku}}), but it is not certain that this ''Daiō'' had been used from the days of Emperor Nintoku, who ruled some 200 years before the compilation of the ''Nihon Shoki''. However, the expression of ''Daiō'' appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Ōjin}} record, and after that, it appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Ingyō}} record, {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku}} record, {{ill|Emperor Kenzō|en|Emperor Kenzō|WD=Q360966|lt=Kenzō}} record and {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Keitai}} record, among others.
There is the inscription {{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 男弟王 在意紫沙加宮時 斯麻 念長寿 遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱 作此鏡」}} (according to Toshio Fukuyama) <ref>{{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 …… 作此鏡」}} can be read roughly: "''In the year of {{lang|ja|癸未}} (Kibi), August, Day 10, Great King (Daiō) year .... this Mirror is made ({{lang|ja|作此鏡}})''". </ref> on the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] (Portrait of person Mirror), owned by [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Wakayama prefecture|Wakayama prefecture]], in which the words {{lang|ja|大王}} and {{lang|ja|男弟王}} appear. From this inscription, in the year ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' ({{lang|ja|癸未}}, ''Kibi'') <ref>See "{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Sexagenary cycle}}". ''Kibi (Mizunoto Hitsuji)'' is the name of year (year of "Water Goat"). There are 60 names of year according to Sexagenary cycle (''Kanshi''). Named year exists in each 60 years. See also [[:ja:%E7%99%B8%E6%9C%AA|ja:Mizunoto Hitsuji]] or [[:fr:Ch%C3%A8vre_d%27eau|French article]].</ref> when the mirror was made, the title ''Daiō'' is supposed to have been used. But there are various interpretations of what year this ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' year was. In several theories, it is: 383, 443, 503, or 623 AD. Among these years, 443 ({{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō}}) or 503 ({{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu}}) are considered most likely. If it is 443, the expression Daiō was used in around the age of Ingyō, in the middle 5th century. However, the characters in the inscription are difficult to interpret. In addition, the content of the inscription itself has various interpretations. The exact years when the expression ''Daiō'' started to be used are unclear.
== See also == * {{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō}} * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}} * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} * [[Five kings of Wa]]
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
[[en:Okimi]]
<!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Order}} began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999th year ）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. --><!--大鷦鷯天皇（おほさざきのすめらみこと）--><!-- Now unsourced In the [[Man'yōshū]], the expression {{lang|ja|大王}} is the most common, and 57 examples appear. Other expressions {{lang|ja|王}}, {{lang|ja|皇}}, {{lang|ja|大王}}, and {{lang|ja|大皇}} also appear. However, all these words are read ''Ōkimi'' or ''Ohokimi'', and there are no examples that the single character {{lang|ja|王}} is read "Kimi". The word {{lang|ja|大君}} is rarely used. There are only a few examples, such as the line {{lang|ja|やすみしし我が大君…}} (Yasumishi shi waga ōkimi...) by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]].--><!-- omitted -- 万葉集には「大王」の表記が最も多く57例が見られ、他に「王」「皇」「大王」「大皇」が見られる。ただしいずれも“オオキミ”“オホキミ”と訓ませており、「王」を単一で“キミ”と訓む例はない。“キミ”に権威を背景とする君主を意味する「君」の字を当てた「大君」は万葉集には柿本人麻呂が詠んだ「やすみしし我が大君…」など数例が見えるのみである。橋本達雄は、記紀歌謡・万葉歌双方に見られるの表現を考察して、枕詞の「やすみしし」は全世界を統治する意で、天皇の称号と同様に道教の教理に基づいて案出されたものと説いている。--><!-- ''Notice:'' This text is the translation of the ja-article "[[:ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)|Ōkimi]]". This was translated by a human being, that means this is '''''not''' machine translation''. If there are strange wording or other problems in this text, they are caused from the fact that the translator is not a native English user. You may correct those strange points, but you should have knowledge of the history of Japan. --> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:اوکیمی]] [[hu:Ókimi]] [[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]] [[ko:치천하대왕]] [[ru:Окими]] [[uk:Окімі]] [[zh:治天下大王]] {{wikidata link|Q718995}} [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Royal titles]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]]
{{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}}
'''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, also read as ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''),<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/天下-577513 Tenka (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 天下 [Tenka (the world under the heaven)]". It says that on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, there is the inscrption "「治天下[獲加多支鹵]大王」(あめのしたしろしめす[わかたけるの]おおきみ, Ame no shita shirosimesu [Wakatakeru no] Ōkimi). "Wakatakeru" is the name of the Ruler of Yamato (Wakoku), called Ōkimi (great king).</ref> was the title of the head of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}, or the {{ill|Monarch|en|Monarch|WD=Q116|lt=Monarch|1=Monarch}} title of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}} (Old Japan).<ref name="nip-okimi">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The first is "the title for a King of {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}". "これらの大王は ....ヤマト政権の王の称号として用いられた".</ref> This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} in {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=ancient Japan|1=History of Japan}}.<ref name="nip-okimi" />
== Etymology == The title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''Ōkimi'' or ''Daiō''), which is an {{ill|Honorific|en|Honorific|WD=Q1326966|lt=honorific title|1=Honorific}} for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[:en:Kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix ''ō'' or ''oho'' which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title "kimi" (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that ''Ōkimi'' is only an honorific form of ''kimi'', a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of {{ill|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|en|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|WD=Q4222379|lt=kango|1=Sino-Japanese vocabulary}} (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999th year ）項目「大王」</ref> There are several instances of the use of ''Ōkimi'', which is understood as a courtesy title of Emperor or royal family.<ref name="nip-okimi-2-3">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi 2,3 (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The second is the title of the substantial ruler of Japan, used in around 6th century. And the third is: A) after the establishment of the title "Tennō", {{lang|ja|大王}} was written like {{lang|ja|大君}} and was read as "ōkimi". B) Title of a member of the royal family during 2nd to 5th century. They were called "ōkimi" and written usually as {{lang|ja|王}} (male) or {{lang|ja|女王}} (female).</ref>

However, some great powers in the region of the {{ill|Yangtze civilization|en|Yangtze civilization|WD=Q856310|lt=Yangtze civilization|1=Yangtze civilization}} did not want to stand in subordinate positions of the nations of the {{ill|Yellow River civilization|en|Yellow River civilization|WD=Q28584|lt=Yellow River civilization|1=Yellow River civilization}} in {{ill|North China|en|North China|WD=Q1046848|lt=North China|1=North China}}, such as {{ill|Chu (state)|en|Chu (state)|WD=Q504759|lt=Chu|1=Chu (state)}}, {{ill|Wu (state)|en|Wu (state)|WD=Q912068|lt=Wu|1=Wu (state)}} and {{ill|Yue (state)|en|Yue (state)|WD=Q1137403|lt=Yue|1=Yue (state)}}. Some of their monarchs titled themselves ''Wang''.

Thus, there were numerous claims to the throne in mainland China. Thereafter, Ying Zheng ({{lang|zh|嬴政}}), the King of [[Qin (state)|Qin]] (Emperor {{ill|Qin Shi Huang|en|Qin Shi Huang|WD=Q7192|lt=Shi Huang|1=Qin Shi Huang}}), who unified China for the first time in 221 BC, adopted the title "Emperor" ({{lang|zh|皇帝}}, ''Huángdì'') instead of title "King", which had been degraded. The King title ''Wang'' became the title granted to subjects of the Emperor, or that assigned to heads of neighboring states who recognized the authority of the Qin Emperor as Master of Tianxia, with a connotation of a subordinate Rank. The latter usage was established thereafter. The monarch of {{ill|Xiongnu|en|Xiongnu|WD=Q188836|lt=Xiongnu|1=Xiongnu}} stood on even ground with the Emperor of Qin, therefore his title was {{ill|Chanyu|en|Chanyu|WD=Q1062502|lt=Chanyu|1=Chanyu}}, not ''Wang''.
=== Early title of ''King of Japan'' === The first appearance of King title related to old Japan is {{lang|ja|漢委奴国王}} (''Kan no Wa no Na no kokuō'', King of Na in Wa of [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Han]]) engraved on the [[King of Na gold seal|gold seal]] which was bestowed on the king of {{ill|Nakoku|en|Nakoku|WD=Q600473|lt=Nakoku|1=Nakoku}} by the emperor {{ill|Emperor Guangwu of Han|en|Emperor Guangwu of Han|WD=Q7268|lt=Guangwu|1=Emperor Guangwu of Han}} of Han in 57 AD.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/漢倭奴国王印-471192 Wa-no-Na-no-Kokuo-In (Kotobank)] See "デジタル大辞泉 -「漢倭奴国王印」". It says "印文は「漢委奴国王」の5字が刻まれている (the inscrption on the seal is 5 letters: Kan Wa Na Koku O)". The seal (and ribbon) were bestowed on king of Nakoku by the emperor Guangwu (光武帝) in 57 AD.</ref>
The word ''Wakoku ō'' (King of Wa) appears in the article dated to the first year of ''Eisho'' (107 AD), in the record of {{ill|Emperor An of Han|en|Emperor An of Han|WD=Q7285|lt=Emperor An|1=Emperor An of Han}} in the ''{{ill|Book of the Later Han|en|Book of the Later Han|WD=Q430428|lt=Book of the Later Han|1=Book of the Later Han}}''. The full title, as written in the record of emperor An, is: "{{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō|1=Suishō}}, the king of Wa, and other". If "Wakoku ō" refers to the King of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}} as a head of the states union, other than a head of small regional state, this description shows the establishment of the {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/帥升-1083317 Suisho (Kotobank)] See "山川 日本史小辞典 改訂新版 -「帥升」". It says Suishō is the king of Wakoku from the late 1st cenrury to the early 2nd century (1世紀末～2世紀初めの倭国の王).</ref>
In addition, [[Himiko]] (c. 180 AD–c. 247 AD) was authorised as the unified Queen of Wakoku (whose capital was {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai koku|1=Yamatai}}) by the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei|1=Cao Wei}} dynasty. There is a theory that the government that existed during Himiko's rule was an early form of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}, though this has been disputed.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/卑弥呼-120826 Himiko (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) -「卑弥呼」", "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「卑弥呼」" and "ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 -「卑弥呼」".</ref>
== Establishment of the ''Daiō (Great King)'' expression == The kanji letters of the title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''ōkimi'', great king) was first appeared in the inscription of the iron sword unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]].<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "百科事典マイペディア 「大王（おおきみ）」".</ref>
On the other hand, on the iron sword which is a silver inlaid sword, unearthed from the Eta Funayama kofun, [[:en:Kumamoto prefecture|Kumamoto prefecture]], there was the inscription. It was difficult to read this inscription. In 1934, Toshio Fukuyama deciphered the inscription and he read it partially as {{lang|ja|「治天下𤟱□□□歯大王世□□」}}. From this reading, Fukuyama identified that ''Daiō'' ({{lang|ja|大王}}) in this script is the [[:en:Emperor Hansho|Emperor Hansho]]. But in 1978, on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]], the inscription was discovered. When Toshio Kishi and other researchers tried to decipher this inscription, they re-examined the reading of the script on the sword from the Eta Funayama kofun. They found the script can be read as {{lang|ja|「治天下獲加多支鹵大王」}}. {{lang|ja|「獲加多支鹵」}} is read "Wakatakeru", and it is the Japanese name of the [[:en:Emperor Yuryaku|Emperor Yuryaku]]. This ''daiō'' inscribed on the sword might be Yuryaku, and this is the first used example of ''Chi Tenka Daiō'', in the late 5th century.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/江田船山古墳-36675 Eta Funayama kofun (Kotobank)] See "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「江田船山古墳」"</ref>
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, there are the scripts "{{lang|ja|大王、風姿…}}" (''Daiō, fūshi''...) in the first Enthronement record of Ohosazaki no Sumeramikoto ({{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}), but it is not certain that this ''Daiō'' had been used from the days of Emperor Nintoku, who ruled some 200 years before the compilation of the ''Nihon Shoki''. However, the expression of ''Daiō'' appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} record, and after that, it appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}} record, {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} record, {{ill|Emperor Kenzō|en|Emperor Kenzō|WD=Q360966|lt=Kenzō|1=Emperor Kenzō}} record and {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} record, among others.
There is the inscription {{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 男弟王 在意紫沙加宮時 斯麻 念長寿 遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱 作此鏡」}} (according to Toshio Fukuyama) <ref>{{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 …… 作此鏡」}} can be read roughly: "''In the year of {{lang|ja|癸未}} (Kibi), August, Day 10, Great King (Daiō) year .... this Mirror is made ({{lang|ja|作此鏡}})''". </ref> on the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] (Portrait of person Mirror), owned by [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Wakayama prefecture|Wakayama prefecture]], in which the words {{lang|ja|大王}} and {{lang|ja|男弟王}} appear. From this inscription, in the year ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' ({{lang|ja|癸未}}, ''Kibi'') <ref>See "{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Sexagenary cycle|1=Sexagenary cycle}}". ''Kibi (Mizunoto Hitsuji)'' is the name of year (year of "Water Goat"). There are 60 names of year according to Sexagenary cycle (''Kanshi''). Named year exists in each 60 years. See also [[:ja:%E7%99%B8%E6%9C%AA|ja:Mizunoto Hitsuji]] or [[:fr:Ch%C3%A8vre_d%27eau|French article]].</ref> when the mirror was made, the title ''Daiō'' is supposed to have been used. But there are various interpretations of what year this ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' year was. In several theories, it is: 383, 443, 503, or 623 AD. Among these years, 443 ({{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}}) or 503 ({{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu|1=Emperor Buretsu}}) are considered most likely. If it is 443, the expression Daiō was used in around the age of Ingyō, in the middle 5th century. However, the characters in the inscription are difficult to interpret. In addition, the content of the inscription itself has various interpretations. The exact years when the expression ''Daiō'' started to be used are unclear.
== See also == * {{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō|1=Suishō}} * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} * [[Five kings of Wa]]
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
[[en:Okimi]]
<!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Order|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999th year ）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. --><!--大鷦鷯天皇（おほさざきのすめらみこと）--><!-- Now unsourced In the [[Man'yōshū]], the expression {{lang|ja|大王}} is the most common, and 57 examples appear. Other expressions {{lang|ja|王}}, {{lang|ja|皇}}, {{lang|ja|大王}}, and {{lang|ja|大皇}} also appear. However, all these words are read ''Ōkimi'' or ''Ohokimi'', and there are no examples that the single character {{lang|ja|王}} is read "Kimi". The word {{lang|ja|大君}} is rarely used. There are only a few examples, such as the line {{lang|ja|やすみしし我が大君…}} (Yasumishi shi waga ōkimi...) by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]].--><!-- omitted -- 万葉集には「大王」の表記が最も多く57例が見られ、他に「王」「皇」「大王」「大皇」が見られる。ただしいずれも“オオキミ”“オホキミ”と訓ませており、「王」を単一で“キミ”と訓む例はない。“キミ”に権威を背景とする君主を意味する「君」の字を当てた「大君」は万葉集には柿本人麻呂が詠んだ「やすみしし我が大君…」など数例が見えるのみである。橋本達雄は、記紀歌謡・万葉歌双方に見られるの表現を考察して、枕詞の「やすみしし」は全世界を統治する意で、天皇の称号と同様に道教の教理に基づいて案出されたものと説いている。--><!-- ''Notice:'' This text is the translation of the ja-article "[[:ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)|Ōkimi]]". This was translated by a human being, that means this is '''''not''' machine translation''. If there are strange wording or other problems in this text, they are caused from the fact that the translator is not a native English user. You may correct those strange points, but you should have knowledge of the history of Japan. --> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:اوکیمی]] [[hu:Ókimi]] [[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]] [[ko:치천하대왕]] [[ru:Окими]] [[uk:Окімі]] [[zh:治天下大王]] {{wikidata link|Q718995}} [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Royal titles]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]]
{{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}}
'''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, also read as ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''),<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/天下-577513 Tenka (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 天下 [Tenka (the world under the heaven)]". It says that on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, there is the inscrption "「治天下[獲加多支鹵]大王」(あめのしたしろしめす[わかたけるの]おおきみ, Ame no shita shirosimesu [Wakatakeru no] Ōkimi). "Wakatakeru" is the name of the Ruler of Yamato (Wakoku), called Ōkimi (great king).</ref> was the title of the head of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}, or the {{ill|Monarch|en|Monarch|WD=Q116|lt=Monarch|1=Monarch}} title of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}} (Old Japan).<ref name="nip-okimi">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The first is "the title for a King of {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}". "これらの大王は ....ヤマト政権の王の称号として用いられた".</ref> This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} in {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=ancient Japan|1=History of Japan}}.<ref name="nip-okimi" />
== Etymology == The title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''Ōkimi'' or ''Daiō''), which is an {{ill|Honorific|en|Honorific|WD=Q1326966|lt=honorific title|1=Honorific}} for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[:en:Kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix ''ō'' or ''oho'' which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title "kimi" (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that ''Ōkimi'' is only an honorific form of ''kimi'', a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of {{ill|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|en|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|WD=Q4222379|lt=kango|1=Sino-Japanese vocabulary}} (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999th year ）項目「大王」</ref> There are several instances of the use of ''Ōkimi'', which is understood as a courtesy title of Emperor or royal family.<ref name="nip-okimi-2-3">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi 2,3 (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The second is the title of the substantial ruler of Japan, used in around 6th century. And the third is: A) after the establishment of the title "Tennō", {{lang|ja|大王}} was written like {{lang|ja|大君}} and was read as "ōkimi". B) Title of a member of the royal family during 2nd to 5th century. They were called "ōkimi" and written usually as {{lang|ja|王}} (male) or {{lang|ja|女王}} (female).</ref>
== Title of ''King of Japan'' in Chinese documents == === ''King'' as a title in ancient China === The {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} title "{{lang|ja|王}}" (''Ō, Wang'') originally designated a Master of ''Chūgen'' ({{lang|ja|中原}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan|en|Zhongyuan|WD=Q190277|lt=Zhongyuan|1=Zhongyuan}}) in {{ill|China proper|en|China proper|WD=Q2079794|lt=Inner China|1=China proper}}. In the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}} period, {{lang|zh|王}} (''Wang'') was the title of the sole {{ill|Son of Heaven|en|Son of Heaven|WD=Q1147432|lt=Son of Heaven|1=Son of Heaven}} who rules the {{ill|Tianxia|en|Tianxia|WD=Q955223|lt=Tianxia|1=Tianxia}}. However, some great powers in the region of the {{ill|Yangtze civilization|en|Yangtze civilization|WD=Q856310|lt=Yangtze civilization|1=Yangtze civilization}} did not want to stand in subordinate positions of the nations of the {{ill|Yellow River civilization|en|Yellow River civilization|WD=Q28584|lt=Yellow River civilization|1=Yellow River civilization}} in {{ill|North China|en|North China|WD=Q1046848|lt=North China|1=North China}}, such as {{ill|Chu (state)|en|Chu (state)|WD=Q504759|lt=Chu|1=Chu (state)}}, {{ill|Wu (state)|en|Wu (state)|WD=Q912068|lt=Wu|1=Wu (state)}} and {{ill|Yue (state)|en|Yue (state)|WD=Q1137403|lt=Yue|1=Yue (state)}}. Some of their monarchs titled themselves ''Wang''.
When China entered into the {{ill|Warring States period|en|Warring States period|WD=Q185063|lt=Warring States period|1=Warring States period}}, the monarchs of the great nations among the {{ill|Ancient Chinese states|en|Ancient Chinese states|WD=Q836688|lt=states of North China|1=Ancient Chinese states}} who were originally subjects of the Zhou King, but achieved territorial statehood, called themselves sole ''Wang'' of the Tianxia in place of the Zhou King. Thus, there were numerous claims to the throne in mainland China. Thereafter, Ying Zheng ({{lang|zh|嬴政}}), the King of [[Qin (state)|Qin]] (Emperor {{ill|Qin Shi Huang|en|Qin Shi Huang|WD=Q7192|lt=Shi Huang|1=Qin Shi Huang}}), who unified China for the first time in 221 BC, adopted the title "Emperor" ({{lang|zh|皇帝}}, ''Huángdì'') instead of title "King", which had been degraded. The King title ''Wang'' became the title granted to subjects of the Emperor, or that assigned to heads of neighboring states who recognized the authority of the Qin Emperor as Master of Tianxia, with a connotation of a subordinate Rank. The latter usage was established thereafter. The monarch of {{ill|Xiongnu|en|Xiongnu|WD=Q188836|lt=Xiongnu|1=Xiongnu}} stood on even ground with the Emperor of Qin, therefore his title was {{ill|Chanyu|en|Chanyu|WD=Q1062502|lt=Chanyu|1=Chanyu}}, not ''Wang''.
=== Early title of ''King of Japan'' === The first appearance of King title related to old Japan is {{lang|ja|漢委奴国王}} (''Kan no Wa no Na no kokuō'', King of Na in Wa of [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Han]]) engraved on the [[King of Na gold seal|gold seal]] which was bestowed on the king of {{ill|Nakoku|en|Nakoku|WD=Q600473|lt=Nakoku|1=Nakoku}} by the emperor {{ill|Emperor Guangwu of Han|en|Emperor Guangwu of Han|WD=Q7268|lt=Guangwu|1=Emperor Guangwu of Han}} of Han in 57 AD.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/漢倭奴国王印-471192 Wa-no-Na-no-Kokuo-In (Kotobank)] See "デジタル大辞泉 -「漢倭奴国王印」". It says "印文は「漢委奴国王」の5字が刻まれている (the inscrption on the seal is 5 letters: Kan Wa Na Koku O)". The seal (and ribbon) were bestowed on king of Nakoku by the emperor Guangwu (光武帝) in 57 AD.</ref>
The word ''Wakoku ō'' (King of Wa) appears in the article dated to the first year of ''Eisho'' (107 AD), in the record of {{ill|Emperor An of Han|en|Emperor An of Han|WD=Q7285|lt=Emperor An|1=Emperor An of Han}} in the ''{{ill|Book of the Later Han|en|Book of the Later Han|WD=Q430428|lt=Book of the Later Han|1=Book of the Later Han}}''. The full title, as written in the record of emperor An, is: "{{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō|1=Suishō}}, the king of Wa, and other". If "Wakoku ō" refers to the King of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}} as a head of the states union, other than a head of small regional state, this description shows the establishment of the {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/帥升-1083317 Suisho (Kotobank)] See "山川 日本史小辞典 改訂新版 -「帥升」". It says Suishō is the king of Wakoku from the late 1st cenrury to the early 2nd century (1世紀末～2世紀初めの倭国の王).</ref>
In addition, [[Himiko]] (c. 180 AD–c. 247 AD) was authorised as the unified Queen of Wakoku (whose capital was {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai koku|1=Yamatai}}) by the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei|1=Cao Wei}} dynasty. There is a theory that the government that existed during Himiko's rule was an early form of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}, though this has been disputed.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/卑弥呼-120826 Himiko (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) -「卑弥呼」", "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「卑弥呼」" and "ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 -「卑弥呼」".</ref>
== Establishment of the ''Daiō (Great King)'' expression == The kanji letters of the title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''ōkimi'', great king) was first appeared in the inscription of the iron sword unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]].<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "百科事典マイペディア 「大王（おおきみ）」".</ref>
On the other hand, on the iron sword which is a silver inlaid sword, unearthed from the Eta Funayama kofun, [[:en:Kumamoto prefecture|Kumamoto prefecture]], there was the inscription. It was difficult to read this inscription. In 1934, Toshio Fukuyama deciphered the inscription and he read it partially as {{lang|ja|「治天下𤟱□□□歯大王世□□」}}. From this reading, Fukuyama identified that ''Daiō'' ({{lang|ja|大王}}) in this script is the [[:en:Emperor Hansho|Emperor Hansho]]. But in 1978, on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]], the inscription was discovered. When Toshio Kishi and other researchers tried to decipher this inscription, they re-examined the reading of the script on the sword from the Eta Funayama kofun. They found the script can be read as {{lang|ja|「治天下獲加多支鹵大王」}}. {{lang|ja|「獲加多支鹵」}} is read "Wakatakeru", and it is the Japanese name of the [[:en:Emperor Yuryaku|Emperor Yuryaku]]. This ''daiō'' inscribed on the sword might be Yuryaku, and this is the first used example of ''Chi Tenka Daiō'', in the late 5th century.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/江田船山古墳-36675 Eta Funayama kofun (Kotobank)] See "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「江田船山古墳」"</ref>
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, there are the scripts "{{lang|ja|大王、風姿…}}" (''Daiō, fūshi''...) in the first Enthronement record of Ohosazaki no Sumeramikoto ({{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}), but it is not certain that this ''Daiō'' had been used from the days of Emperor Nintoku, who ruled some 200 years before the compilation of the ''Nihon Shoki''. However, the expression of ''Daiō'' appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} record, and after that, it appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}} record, {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} record, {{ill|Emperor Kenzō|en|Emperor Kenzō|WD=Q360966|lt=Kenzō|1=Emperor Kenzō}} record and {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} record, among others.
There is the inscription {{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 男弟王 在意紫沙加宮時 斯麻 念長寿 遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱 作此鏡」}} (according to Toshio Fukuyama) <ref>{{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 …… 作此鏡」}} can be read roughly: "''In the year of {{lang|ja|癸未}} (Kibi), August, Day 10, Great King (Daiō) year .... this Mirror is made ({{lang|ja|作此鏡}})''". </ref> on the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] (Portrait of person Mirror), owned by [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Wakayama prefecture|Wakayama prefecture]], in which the words {{lang|ja|大王}} and {{lang|ja|男弟王}} appear. From this inscription, in the year ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' ({{lang|ja|癸未}}, ''Kibi'') <ref>See "{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Sexagenary cycle|1=Sexagenary cycle}}". ''Kibi (Mizunoto Hitsuji)'' is the name of year (year of "Water Goat"). There are 60 names of year according to Sexagenary cycle (''Kanshi''). Named year exists in each 60 years. See also [[:ja:%E7%99%B8%E6%9C%AA|ja:Mizunoto Hitsuji]] or [[:fr:Ch%C3%A8vre_d%27eau|French article]].</ref> when the mirror was made, the title ''Daiō'' is supposed to have been used. But there are various interpretations of what year this ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' year was. In several theories, it is: 383, 443, 503, or 623 AD. Among these years, 443 ({{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}}) or 503 ({{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu|1=Emperor Buretsu}}) are considered most likely. If it is 443, the expression Daiō was used in around the age of Ingyō, in the middle 5th century. However, the characters in the inscription are difficult to interpret. In addition, the content of the inscription itself has various interpretations. The exact years when the expression ''Daiō'' started to be used are unclear.
== See also == * {{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō|1=Suishō}} * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} * [[Five kings of Wa]]
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
[[en:Okimi]]
<!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Order|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999th year ）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. --><!--大鷦鷯天皇（おほさざきのすめらみこと）--><!-- Now unsourced In the [[Man'yōshū]], the expression {{lang|ja|大王}} is the most common, and 57 examples appear. Other expressions {{lang|ja|王}}, {{lang|ja|皇}}, {{lang|ja|大王}}, and {{lang|ja|大皇}} also appear. However, all these words are read ''Ōkimi'' or ''Ohokimi'', and there are no examples that the single character {{lang|ja|王}} is read "Kimi". The word {{lang|ja|大君}} is rarely used. There are only a few examples, such as the line {{lang|ja|やすみしし我が大君…}} (Yasumishi shi waga ōkimi...) by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]].--><!-- omitted -- 万葉集には「大王」の表記が最も多く57例が見られ、他に「王」「皇」「大王」「大皇」が見られる。ただしいずれも“オオキミ”“オホキミ”と訓ませており、「王」を単一で“キミ”と訓む例はない。“キミ”に権威を背景とする君主を意味する「君」の字を当てた「大君」は万葉集には柿本人麻呂が詠んだ「やすみしし我が大君…」など数例が見えるのみである。橋本達雄は、記紀歌謡・万葉歌双方に見られるの表現を考察して、枕詞の「やすみしし」は全世界を統治する意で、天皇の称号と同様に道教の教理に基づいて案出されたものと説いている。--><!-- ''Notice:'' This text is the translation of the ja-article "[[:ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)|Ōkimi]]". This was translated by a human being, that means this is '''''not''' machine translation''. If there are strange wording or other problems in this text, they are caused from the fact that the translator is not a native English user. You may correct those strange points, but you should have knowledge of the history of Japan. --> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:اوکیمی]] [[hu:Ókimi]] [[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]] [[ko:치천하대왕]] [[ru:Окими]] [[uk:Окімі]] [[zh:治天下大王]] {{wikidata link|Q718995}} [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Royal titles]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Okimi]][[fa:اوکیمی]][[hu:Ókimi]][[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]][[ko:치천하대왕]][[ru:Окими]][[uk:Окімі]][[zh:治天下大王]]
{{Short description|Japanese rulers' title}}
'''Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|大王}}, also read as ''Daiō''), or '''Ame no shita Siroshimesu Ōkimi''' ({{lang|ja|治天下大王}}, '''Chi Tenka Daiō'''),<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/天下-577513 Tenka (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 天下 [Tenka (the world under the heaven)]". It says that on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, there is the inscrption "「治天下[獲加多支鹵]大王」(あめのしたしろしめす[わかたけるの]おおきみ, Ame no shita shirosimesu [Wakatakeru no] Ōkimi). "Wakatakeru" is the name of the Ruler of Yamato (Wakoku), called Ōkimi (great king).</ref> was the title of the head of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}, or the {{ill|Monarch|en|Monarch|WD=Q116|lt=Monarch|1=Monarch}} title of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}} (Old Japan).<ref name="nip-okimi">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The first is "the title for a King of {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}". "これらの大王は ....ヤマト政権の王の称号として用いられた".</ref> This term was used from the [[Kofun period]] through the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} in {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=ancient Japan|1=History of Japan}}.<ref name="nip-okimi" />
== Etymology == The title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''Ōkimi'' or ''Daiō''), which is an {{ill|Honorific|en|Honorific|WD=Q1326966|lt=honorific title|1=Honorific}} for the head (king) of the Yamato Kingship, was established around the 5th century and was used until the 680s. It was established when the compilation of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} started. There are several theories upon whether the title holder in the early period is a king of the unified kingship or not. ''Ōkimi'' in [[:en:Kun-yomi|Japanese reading]] is created by adding the prefix ''ō'' or ''oho'' which indicates greatness and particular nobleness, to the title "kimi" (lord), which indicates a master or nobleman. Another theory states that ''Ōkimi'' is only an honorific form of ''kimi'', a title with Japanese origins, while ''Daiō'' is based on a king title which originated from China; the title of {{ill|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|en|Sino-Japanese vocabulary|WD=Q4222379|lt=kango|1=Sino-Japanese vocabulary}} (Chinese word).<ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999th year ）項目「大王」</ref> There are several instances of the use of ''Ōkimi'', which is understood as a courtesy title of Emperor or royal family.<ref name="nip-okimi-2-3">[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi 2,3 (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) - 大王（尊称）[Ōkimi (honorific title)]". It says there were 3 usages for Okimi. The second is the title of the substantial ruler of Japan, used in around 6th century. And the third is: A) after the establishment of the title "Tennō", {{lang|ja|大王}} was written like {{lang|ja|大君}} and was read as "ōkimi". B) Title of a member of the royal family during 2nd to 5th century. They were called "ōkimi" and written usually as {{lang|ja|王}} (male) or {{lang|ja|女王}} (female).</ref>
== Title of ''King of Japan'' in Chinese documents == === ''King'' as a title in ancient China === The {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} title "{{lang|ja|王}}" (''Ō, Wang'') originally designated a Master of ''Chūgen'' ({{lang|ja|中原}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan|en|Zhongyuan|WD=Q190277|lt=Zhongyuan|1=Zhongyuan}}) in {{ill|China proper|en|China proper|WD=Q2079794|lt=Inner China|1=China proper}}. In the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}} period, {{lang|zh|王}} (''Wang'') was the title of the sole {{ill|Son of Heaven|en|Son of Heaven|WD=Q1147432|lt=Son of Heaven|1=Son of Heaven}} who rules the {{ill|Tianxia|en|Tianxia|WD=Q955223|lt=Tianxia|1=Tianxia}}. However, some great powers in the region of the {{ill|Yangtze civilization|en|Yangtze civilization|WD=Q856310|lt=Yangtze civilization|1=Yangtze civilization}} did not want to stand in subordinate positions of the nations of the {{ill|Yellow River civilization|en|Yellow River civilization|WD=Q28584|lt=Yellow River civilization|1=Yellow River civilization}} in {{ill|North China|en|North China|WD=Q1046848|lt=North China|1=North China}}, such as {{ill|Chu (state)|en|Chu (state)|WD=Q504759|lt=Chu|1=Chu (state)}}, {{ill|Wu (state)|en|Wu (state)|WD=Q912068|lt=Wu|1=Wu (state)}} and {{ill|Yue (state)|en|Yue (state)|WD=Q1137403|lt=Yue|1=Yue (state)}}. Some of their monarchs titled themselves ''Wang''.
When China entered into the {{ill|Warring States period|en|Warring States period|WD=Q185063|lt=Warring States period|1=Warring States period}}, the monarchs of the great nations among the {{ill|Ancient Chinese states|en|Ancient Chinese states|WD=Q836688|lt=states of North China|1=Ancient Chinese states}} who were originally subjects of the Zhou King, but achieved territorial statehood, called themselves sole ''Wang'' of the Tianxia in place of the Zhou King. Thus, there were numerous claims to the throne in mainland China. Thereafter, Ying Zheng ({{lang|zh|嬴政}}), the King of [[Qin (state)|Qin]] (Emperor {{ill|Qin Shi Huang|en|Qin Shi Huang|WD=Q7192|lt=Shi Huang|1=Qin Shi Huang}}), who unified China for the first time in 221 BC, adopted the title "Emperor" ({{lang|zh|皇帝}}, ''Huángdì'') instead of title "King", which had been degraded. The King title ''Wang'' became the title granted to subjects of the Emperor, or that assigned to heads of neighboring states who recognized the authority of the Qin Emperor as Master of Tianxia, with a connotation of a subordinate Rank. The latter usage was established thereafter. The monarch of {{ill|Xiongnu|en|Xiongnu|WD=Q188836|lt=Xiongnu|1=Xiongnu}} stood on even ground with the Emperor of Qin, therefore his title was {{ill|Chanyu|en|Chanyu|WD=Q1062502|lt=Chanyu|1=Chanyu}}, not ''Wang''.
=== Early title of ''King of Japan'' === The first appearance of King title related to old Japan is {{lang|ja|漢委奴国王}} (''Kan no Wa no Na no kokuō'', King of Na in Wa of [[Han dynasty#Eastern Han|Han]]) engraved on the [[King of Na gold seal|gold seal]] which was bestowed on the king of {{ill|Nakoku|en|Nakoku|WD=Q600473|lt=Nakoku|1=Nakoku}} by the emperor {{ill|Emperor Guangwu of Han|en|Emperor Guangwu of Han|WD=Q7268|lt=Guangwu|1=Emperor Guangwu of Han}} of Han in 57 AD.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/漢倭奴国王印-471192 Wa-no-Na-no-Kokuo-In (Kotobank)] See "デジタル大辞泉 -「漢倭奴国王印」". It says "印文は「漢委奴国王」の5字が刻まれている (the inscrption on the seal is 5 letters: Kan Wa Na Koku O)". The seal (and ribbon) were bestowed on king of Nakoku by the emperor Guangwu (光武帝) in 57 AD.</ref>
The word ''Wakoku ō'' (King of Wa) appears in the article dated to the first year of ''Eisho'' (107 AD), in the record of {{ill|Emperor An of Han|en|Emperor An of Han|WD=Q7285|lt=Emperor An|1=Emperor An of Han}} in the ''{{ill|Book of the Later Han|en|Book of the Later Han|WD=Q430428|lt=Book of the Later Han|1=Book of the Later Han}}''. The full title, as written in the record of emperor An, is: "{{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō|1=Suishō}}, the king of Wa, and other". If "Wakoku ō" refers to the King of {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}} as a head of the states union, other than a head of small regional state, this description shows the establishment of the {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|WD=Q10888424|lt=Wakoku|1=Wakoku}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/帥升-1083317 Suisho (Kotobank)] See "山川 日本史小辞典 改訂新版 -「帥升」". It says Suishō is the king of Wakoku from the late 1st cenrury to the early 2nd century (1世紀末～2世紀初めの倭国の王).</ref>
In addition, [[Himiko]] (c. 180 AD–c. 247 AD) was authorised as the unified Queen of Wakoku (whose capital was {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai koku|1=Yamatai}}) by the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei|1=Cao Wei}} dynasty. There is a theory that the government that existed during Himiko's rule was an early form of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}}, though this has been disputed.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/卑弥呼-120826 Himiko (Kotobank)] See "日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) -「卑弥呼」", "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「卑弥呼」" and "ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 -「卑弥呼」".</ref>
== Establishment of the ''Daiō (Great King)'' expression == The kanji letters of the title {{lang|ja|大王}} (''ōkimi'', great king) was first appeared in the inscription of the iron sword unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]].<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大王-90837 Okimi (Kotobank)] See "百科事典マイペディア 「大王（おおきみ）」".</ref>
On the other hand, on the iron sword which is a silver inlaid sword, unearthed from the Eta Funayama kofun, [[:en:Kumamoto prefecture|Kumamoto prefecture]], there was the inscription. It was difficult to read this inscription. In 1934, Toshio Fukuyama deciphered the inscription and he read it partially as {{lang|ja|「治天下𤟱□□□歯大王世□□」}}. From this reading, Fukuyama identified that ''Daiō'' ({{lang|ja|大王}}) in this script is the [[:en:Emperor Hansho|Emperor Hansho]]. But in 1978, on the iron sword, unearthed from Inariyama kofun, [[:en:Saitama prefecture|Saitama prefecture]], the inscription was discovered. When Toshio Kishi and other researchers tried to decipher this inscription, they re-examined the reading of the script on the sword from the Eta Funayama kofun. They found the script can be read as {{lang|ja|「治天下獲加多支鹵大王」}}. {{lang|ja|「獲加多支鹵」}} is read "Wakatakeru", and it is the Japanese name of the [[:en:Emperor Yuryaku|Emperor Yuryaku]]. This ''daiō'' inscribed on the sword might be Yuryaku, and this is the first used example of ''Chi Tenka Daiō'', in the late 5th century.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/江田船山古墳-36675 Eta Funayama kofun (Kotobank)] See "改訂新版 世界大百科事典 -「江田船山古墳」"</ref>
In ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', compiled in the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, there are the scripts "{{lang|ja|大王、風姿…}}" (''Daiō, fūshi''...) in the first Enthronement record of Ohosazaki no Sumeramikoto ({{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}), but it is not certain that this ''Daiō'' had been used from the days of Emperor Nintoku, who ruled some 200 years before the compilation of the ''Nihon Shoki''. However, the expression of ''Daiō'' appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} record, and after that, it appears in the {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}} record, {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} record, {{ill|Emperor Kenzō|en|Emperor Kenzō|WD=Q360966|lt=Kenzō|1=Emperor Kenzō}} record and {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} record, among others.
There is the inscription {{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 男弟王 在意紫沙加宮時 斯麻 念長寿 遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱 作此鏡」}} (according to Toshio Fukuyama) <ref>{{lang|ja|「癸未年八月日十 大王年 …… 作此鏡」}} can be read roughly: "''In the year of {{lang|ja|癸未}} (Kibi), August, Day 10, Great King (Daiō) year .... this Mirror is made ({{lang|ja|作此鏡}})''". </ref> on the [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] (Portrait of person Mirror), owned by [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Wakayama prefecture|Wakayama prefecture]], in which the words {{lang|ja|大王}} and {{lang|ja|男弟王}} appear. From this inscription, in the year ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' ({{lang|ja|癸未}}, ''Kibi'') <ref>See "{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Sexagenary cycle|1=Sexagenary cycle}}". ''Kibi (Mizunoto Hitsuji)'' is the name of year (year of "Water Goat"). There are 60 names of year according to Sexagenary cycle (''Kanshi''). Named year exists in each 60 years. See also [[:ja:%E7%99%B8%E6%9C%AA|ja:Mizunoto Hitsuji]] or [[:fr:Ch%C3%A8vre_d%27eau|French article]].</ref> when the mirror was made, the title ''Daiō'' is supposed to have been used. But there are various interpretations of what year this ''Mizunoto Hitsuji'' year was. In several theories, it is: 383, 443, 503, or 623 AD. Among these years, 443 ({{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō|1=Emperor Ingyō}}) or 503 ({{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Emperor Buretsu|1=Emperor Buretsu}}) are considered most likely. If it is 443, the expression Daiō was used in around the age of Ingyō, in the middle 5th century. However, the characters in the inscription are difficult to interpret. In addition, the content of the inscription itself has various interpretations. The exact years when the expression ''Daiō'' started to be used are unclear.
== See also == * {{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|WD=Q3276192|lt=Suishō|1=Suishō}} * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} * [[Five kings of Wa]]
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
[[en:Okimi]]
<!-- Around the 5th century, the title '''Daioh''' was established as the title of the chief (king) of the [[Yamato period|Yamato Kingdom]], and was used in Japan until the [[680]] dynasty, when the compilation of the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Order|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}} began. In the early days, theories were divided as to whether the king was a king of a unified kingdom or not. In the early days, there were different theories as to whether he was the king of the unified kingdom. On the other hand, some say that this is just a Japanese term for "kimi" as a title of respect, and that "daiou" is a Chinese title originating from the gift of a royal title from China. <ref>永原慶二監修『岩波日本史辞典』（岩波書店、1999th year ）「大王」の項。</ref>。There are many instances of the word "daiō" being used as a term of endearment for the emperor or the royal family. --><!--大鷦鷯天皇（おほさざきのすめらみこと）--><!-- Now unsourced In the [[Man'yōshū]], the expression {{lang|ja|大王}} is the most common, and 57 examples appear. Other expressions {{lang|ja|王}}, {{lang|ja|皇}}, {{lang|ja|大王}}, and {{lang|ja|大皇}} also appear. However, all these words are read ''Ōkimi'' or ''Ohokimi'', and there are no examples that the single character {{lang|ja|王}} is read "Kimi". The word {{lang|ja|大君}} is rarely used. There are only a few examples, such as the line {{lang|ja|やすみしし我が大君…}} (Yasumishi shi waga ōkimi...) by [[Kakinomoto no Hitomaro]].--><!-- omitted -- 万葉集には「大王」の表記が最も多く57例が見られ、他に「王」「皇」「大王」「大皇」が見られる。ただしいずれも“オオキミ”“オホキミ”と訓ませており、「王」を単一で“キミ”と訓む例はない。“キミ”に権威を背景とする君主を意味する「君」の字を当てた「大君」は万葉集には柿本人麻呂が詠んだ「やすみしし我が大君…」など数例が見えるのみである。橋本達雄は、記紀歌謡・万葉歌双方に見られるの表現を考察して、枕詞の「やすみしし」は全世界を統治する意で、天皇の称号と同様に道教の教理に基づいて案出されたものと説いている。--><!-- ''Notice:'' This text is the translation of the ja-article "[[:ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)|Ōkimi]]". This was translated by a human being, that means this is '''''not''' machine translation''. If there are strange wording or other problems in this text, they are caused from the fact that the translator is not a native English user. You may correct those strange points, but you should have knowledge of the history of Japan. --> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:اوکیمی]] [[hu:Ókimi]] [[ja:大王 (ヤマト王権)]] [[ko:치천하대왕]] [[ru:Окими]] [[uk:Окімі]] [[zh:治天下大王]] {{wikidata link|Q718995}} [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Royal titles]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]
